Binary indicator of any irregular rhythm in 0–12h (e.g., any AF episode). Presence of early AF is a plausible AKI risk proxy via hemodynamics, but it is common post-op and ‘max’ presence is coarse.
Binary indicator of any irregular rhythm during 13–24h. Captures occurrence but not duration/severity; provides only weak-to-moderate indirect information about renal perfusion risk.

Irregular cardiac rhythm (e.g., AF/ectopy) in the 25–36h window can signal hemodynamic instability and reduced renal perfusion. It is an indirect but clinically relevant AKI risk marker; max captures any occurrence but remains nonspecific to kidney injury.

Early postoperative irregular rhythm is common after cardiac surgery and often transient. As a minimum/absence-type summary it is less sensitive to brief arrhythmic insults and is only an indirect renal risk proxy.

Irregular rhythm in 13–24h may reflect evolving instability, but the min aggregation under-captures intermittent arrhythmia burden. Renal linkage is indirect (perfusion/vasopressor needs), so predictive value is modest.

Late-window irregular rhythm could matter for renal perfusion, but the min aggregation likely reflects sustained absence/presence poorly for intermittent arrhythmias. Overall it is a nonspecific hemodynamic proxy rather than a renal biomarker.

Count/frequency of documenting irregular rhythm is primarily monitoring intensity and workflow rather than physiology. Early post-op measurement frequency is especially confounded by routine surveillance, with weak renal specificity.
Measurement frequency of rhythm irregularity mainly reflects clinician attention/monitoring patterns. It is not a direct renal mechanism and is a weak, proxy-heavy predictor of creatinine rise.

More frequent documentation of irregular rhythm late may indicate ongoing instability prompting closer monitoring, which can correlate with AKI risk. Still, it is largely a care-process proxy, so it should not score high.

“Other” rhythm regularity category is ambiguous and likely mixes heterogeneous rhythms and documentation artifacts. Mean in the immediate post-op window is nonspecific and poorly tied to renal injury pathways.

A binary/mean indicator of an “other” rhythm category is difficult to interpret clinically and likely reflects coding variability. Renal relevance is indirect at best and adds little beyond more specific hemodynamic variables.

If “other” captures paced rhythm or significant conduction abnormalities, it may correlate with low output states affecting renal perfusion in the 25–36h window. However, heterogeneity and mean aggregation limit specificity and signal quality.
Any occurrence of an “other” rhythm early after surgery is common and often reflects transient pacing/monitoring labels. It is nonspecific and weakly linked to subsequent creatinine ratio.

Occurrence of an “other” rhythm in 13–24h could indicate persistent pacing or conduction issues associated with hemodynamic compromise. Still, the category is vague and remains an indirect renal risk marker.

Any “other” rhythm late may reflect ongoing pacing/conduction abnormality and potential low cardiac output affecting renal perfusion. The signal is indirect and category ambiguity limits predictive strength.
Minimum/absence-type summary of an ambiguous “other” rhythm category in the immediate post-op period is unlikely to capture meaningful pathology. Renal specificity is very low.

Min aggregation for a heterogeneous “other” rhythm label provides little interpretable physiologic information. Any renal association would be indirect and better captured by direct hemodynamics/vasopressors.

Even in the closest window, a minimum summary of an ill-defined rhythm category is unlikely to add actionable signal for AKI prediction. High proxy/measurement noise and low renal specificity justify a low score.

Frequency of documenting an 'other' cardiac rhythm/regularity category in the immediate postop window; largely a monitoring/documentation artifact. Minimal direct renal specificity and high proxy penalty for clinician attention rather than kidney physiology.
Measurement count of an 'other' rhythm label during 13–24h; reflects documentation/monitoring intensity more than a renal mechanism. Weak and indirect link to AKI risk, so low importance.

Measurement frequency of an atypical rhythm category closer to the outcome window may weakly proxy ongoing instability/arrhythmia burden. Still primarily a process/attention signal (_measured) with limited renal specificity, so capped low.

Mean heart rate from pulse-ox monitoring in the first 12h; tachycardia can reflect stress, pain, bleeding, or low output that may affect renal perfusion. Early postop means are often transient and nonspecific, so only weak predictive value for creatinine ratio.

Mean monitored HR in 13–24h may reflect persistent sympathetic drive or evolving low cardiac output, indirectly relevant to renal perfusion. Mean aggregation can dilute brief but important episodes, keeping importance modest.

Mean monitored HR in 25–36h is temporally closer and may capture sustained tachycardia from ongoing shock/low output that can precede AKI. Still indirect (not kidney-specific) and mean-based, so moderate rather than high.

Peak monitored HR in 0–12h captures extremes (e.g., atrial fibrillation with RVR, pain, hypovolemia) that can correlate with hemodynamic stress. Immediate postop peaks are common and nonspecific, limiting renal prognostic proximity.

Peak HR in 13–24h better reflects persistent or recurrent instability (arrhythmia, low output) that can impair renal perfusion. Extreme aggregation is appropriate for capturing insults, giving moderate importance.
Peak HR in 25–36h is a closer leading indicator of ongoing hemodynamic stress that may precede worsening creatinine. Still an indirect proxy (not a renal biomarker), so not top-tier.

Minimum monitored HR in 0–12h may reflect bradycardia, pacing, deep sedation, or conduction issues; renal relevance is indirect via cardiac output. Early postoperative bradycardia is often iatrogenic/transient, so weak predictor.

Minimum HR in 13–24h could indicate conduction disease, pacing dependence, or medication effects with potential low-output implications. However it is nonspecific and often treatment-related, so limited renal specificity.
Minimum HR in 25–36h is closer to outcome but still commonly reflects pacing/sedation/medication rather than intrinsic hypoperfusion. Indirect association with AKI keeps importance low.
Count/frequency of HR measurements from pulse-ox monitoring in 0–12h primarily reflects monitoring intensity (often routine continuous monitoring). High proxy penalty and minimal renal specificity.
Measurement frequency of monitored HR in 13–24h is largely a care-process variable and typically continuous by default. Weak relationship to kidney injury beyond general acuity.
In 25–36h, increased measurement frequency may weakly signal clinician concern for instability, which can correlate with AKI risk. Still a _measured proxy feature, so kept low.

Mean apical (manually assessed) heart rate in 0–12h may indicate arrhythmia evaluation or unreliable monitors, indirectly tied to hemodynamic status. Early timing and nonspecific renal linkage limit predictive usefulness.

Mean apical heart rate in the 13–24h window reflects overall sympathetic tone/illness and possibly low-output states. It is an indirect proxy for renal perfusion and the mean can wash out brief hypotensive/low-flow episodes, so predictive value for creatinine rise is limited.

Mean apical heart rate closer to the outcome may reflect sustained stress, pain, fever, arrhythmia, or low cardiac output. Still nonspecific for kidney injury and mean aggregation dilutes short critical insults, so only weak-to-moderate renal relevance.

Indicator of a high apical heart rate early post-op is common due to anesthesia emergence, catecholamines, and pain. It is nonspecific and temporally early, making it a weak predictor of Hour-60 creatinine ratio.
Peak apical heart rate in 13–24h may capture episodes of tachyarrhythmia or stress that can reduce renal perfusion. It remains indirect and confounded by treatment/fever, so modest usefulness.

Peak apical heart rate in 25–36h is temporally closer and may reflect ongoing shock/arrhythmia affecting renal blood flow. However it is still a nonspecific hemodynamic proxy without direct renal specificity.

Indicator of low apical heart rate in the immediate post-op period often reflects sedation, pacing settings, or transient conduction issues. It is an indirect and early signal with substantial clinician-management confounding.

Lowest apical heart rate in 13–24h could reflect bradyarrhythmia/low-output episodes that may impair renal perfusion. Still indirect and often influenced by pacing/medications, limiting renal-specific prognostic value.

Lowest apical heart rate in 25–36h is closer to the creatinine outcome and could mark clinically meaningful low-output/bradycardic periods. Nonetheless it is a nonspecific proxy and frequently treatment-driven (pacing, beta-blockade).
Count/frequency of apical HR measurements mainly reflects monitoring intensity rather than physiology. Early post-op measurement frequency is routine and not kidney-specific.

Apical HR measurement frequency is largely a care-process variable and may reflect nursing workflow or concern. It has minimal direct linkage to renal injury pathways and should be heavily proxy-penalized.
More frequent apical HR checks in 25–36h may weakly signal instability prompting closer monitoring. Still primarily a clinician-behavior proxy and not a direct renal mechanism, so capped low.

Mean peripheral heart rate in 0–12h is heavily influenced by immediate post-bypass physiology, sedation, and routine ICU care. It is nonspecific and temporally early, offering little direct information about evolving AKI.

Peripheral mean heart rate in 13–24h may reflect ongoing stress or low cardiac output affecting renal perfusion. It remains indirect and redundant with apical HR summaries, so only weak predictive value.

Peripheral mean heart rate in 25–36h is closer to the outcome and may capture sustained physiologic stress relevant to renal perfusion. However it is nonspecific and mean aggregation reduces sensitivity to brief critical events.
Peak peripheral heart rate in the immediate post-op window is common and often transient (pain, catecholamines, emergence). It is a weak, nonspecific proxy for renal risk at Hour 60.
Peak peripheral heart rate in 13–24h may capture tachyarrhythmia or stress episodes that can compromise renal perfusion. Still indirect and confounded by fever/medications, so modest importance.

Binary indicator that the heart rate source was peripheral (monitoring source/lead), not a physiologic HR value. This is largely a documentation/monitoring artifact with minimal renal mechanistic relevance despite being in the closest window.
Indicates peripheral source for HR measurement in the immediate postop period, reflecting monitoring setup rather than hemodynamics. Strong proxy penalty and no direct link to AKI or creatinine rise.
Peripheral HR source flag during 13–24h is a care-process/monitoring characteristic. It does not capture renal perfusion or injury and is unlikely to add predictive value beyond general ICU context.

Peripheral HR source flag in 25–36h remains a monitoring-source variable rather than a physiologic insult marker. Even with temporal proximity, it has negligible renal specificity and high proxy penalty.
Count/frequency of documenting peripheral HR source in 0–12h reflects monitoring intensity and charting patterns. Per rules, _measured variables are weak proxies and not renal-pathway indicators.

Measurement frequency of a monitoring-source field (peripheral HR source) is primarily clinician/EMR behavior. It has low renal specificity and limited prognostic proximity to Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

Frequency of recording peripheral HR source in the 25–36h window is still a monitoring-intensity proxy rather than physiology. Even near the outcome, it is unlikely to meaningfully predict creatinine trajectory.

Proportion of time on mechanical ventilation in the first 12h reflects immediate postop respiratory support and overall acuity. Renal relevance is indirect (severity/positive pressure effects), and early window derangements are common/transient.

Average mechanical ventilation use in 13–24h suggests slower recovery and higher illness severity, which can correlate with AKI risk. Still indirect and partly treatment-practice dependent, so only weak-to-moderate utility.

Sustained mechanical ventilation in 25–36h is a closer marker of ongoing critical illness and potential hemodynamic/venous congestion effects that can worsen renal function. Mean exposure here can be informative for persistent instability near Hour 60, though still indirect.
Any mechanical ventilation in 0–12h is expected after cardiac surgery and mainly reflects routine postop management. Limited renal specificity and strong confounding by standard care pathways.

Any mechanical ventilation during 13–24h indicates delayed extubation and higher acuity, which can associate with AKI. However it is a coarse binary proxy and not a direct renal perfusion/injury marker.

Any mechanical ventilation in 25–36h implies persistent respiratory failure/critical illness close to the outcome window, which often co-travels with shock, congestion, and nephrotoxic exposures. Still a proxy variable, but temporally proximal and clinically meaningful.

Minimum mechanical ventilation status over 0–12h largely captures whether the patient was ever off the ventilator, which is not renal-specific and is heavily influenced by routine extubation timing. Poor aggregation for renal pathology in the immediate postop window.

Minimum ventilation status in 13–24h approximates whether the patient achieved any time off the ventilator, reflecting recovery trajectory. Indirect renal relevance via overall physiologic recovery, but still coarse and practice-dependent.

Minimum ventilation status in 25–36h indicates whether the patient had any period off mechanical support, a recovery proxy near the outcome. Renal linkage remains indirect and the min aggregation is less informative than sustained exposure (mean) for ongoing critical illness.

Counts/flags documentation of mechanical ventilation in the immediate postop window; this is largely monitoring/therapy intensity rather than a renal mechanism. Early postop ventilation is common and nonspecific for AKI risk, so it has low direct value for Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

Measurement frequency of being on mechanical ventilation during 13–24h mainly reflects ongoing respiratory support and illness severity. It is an indirect proxy for hemodynamic stress/inflammation with limited renal specificity, but more informative than 0–12h.

Measurement frequency of mechanical ventilation in the 25–36h window suggests persistent critical illness closer to the outcome. Still primarily a care-process/acuity proxy (not renal-specific), so it should not score high.

Mean time on no oxygen device in the first 12h reflects early extubation/low respiratory support, which is common and heavily influenced by anesthesia and practice patterns. It has weak and indirect linkage to renal injury pathways.

Average time on room air/no device during 13–24h suggests recovery and lower overall physiologic stress. It is still nonspecific for renal perfusion/injury and can be confounded by extubation practices.

Mean time on no oxygen device at 25–36h indicates clinical stability closer to Hour 60 and may correlate with lower AKI risk via less shock/organ failure. However it remains an indirect proxy with substantial practice-pattern confounding.

Binary max indicates whether the patient was ever on no oxygen device in 0–12h (e.g., brief room-air periods). This is highly practice-dependent and not renal-specific, and early transient states are common post-op.
Whether the patient ever reached no oxygen device in 13–24h captures a milestone of respiratory recovery. It may weakly track lower systemic stress but is still indirect for AKI prediction.

Ever being on no oxygen device in 25–36h suggests stability near the outcome window. It remains a nonspecific proxy (respiratory/overall recovery) rather than a direct renal perfusion or injury marker.

Binary min indicates whether the patient was on no oxygen device for the entire 0–12h window, which is uncommon and strongly influenced by immediate postop workflow. It has minimal renal specificity and high confounding.
Being on no oxygen device throughout 13–24h implies sustained respiratory stability and lower overall acuity. This can correlate with less AKI risk but remains indirect and not kidney-mechanistic.

Sustained room air/no device across 25–36h is a stronger stability signal close to Hour 60. Still, it is a general recovery marker with limited renal specificity, so it should not be scored above moderate.

Measurement frequency of 'no oxygen device' is primarily documentation/monitoring intensity rather than physiology. In the immediate postop period it is especially confounded by routine charting and offers little renal signal.
How often 'no oxygen device' is recorded during 13–24h mainly reflects charting patterns and monitoring intensity. It has weak renal relevance and should be kept low per the _measured proxy penalty.
Frequency of documenting 'no oxygen device' at 25–36h is still a care-process proxy and not a renal biomarker. Even though temporally closer, _measured variables should remain low importance.

Mean use of low-flow oxygen in 0–12h reflects early postoperative respiratory support needs and overall physiologic stress. It is an indirect AKI risk proxy (hypoxia/illness severity) but early timing and nonspecificity limit importance.

Represents average use of low-flow oxygen (nasal cannula/simple mask), mainly reflecting respiratory support needs and overall recovery. Only indirectly related to AKI via general illness severity and oxygenation/perfusion proxies, with mean aggregation and mid-epoch timing limiting renal specificity.

Average low-flow oxygen requirement in the 25–36h window suggests ongoing but relatively mild respiratory support. Closer timing helps slightly, but it remains a nonspecific proxy for overall status rather than a renal mechanism.

Any use of low-flow oxygen early post-op is common and heavily influenced by routine extubation/weaning practices. It has minimal renal specificity and is largely a care-process/trajectory proxy in the immediate post-op period.

Indicates whether low-flow oxygen was used at any point in 13–24h, reflecting respiratory support needs after initial stabilization. Still an indirect severity marker with weak linkage to renal perfusion or kidney injury pathways.

Any low-flow oxygen use in 25–36h may reflect persistent cardiopulmonary limitation closer to the creatinine outcome. However, it remains nonspecific and is more a proxy for overall recovery than a direct AKI predictor.

Minimum (i.e., whether low-flow oxygen was ever absent) in 0–12h is dominated by immediate post-op ventilation/extubation workflow. It provides little renal-relevant signal and is largely process-driven.

Minimum low-flow oxygen use in 13–24h is hard to interpret clinically and is redundant with other oxygen-device indicators. It is a weak, indirect proxy with poor aggregation relevance to renal injury.
Minimum low-flow oxygen use in 25–36h is similarly difficult to map to a meaningful physiologic insult and is redundant with max/mean indicators. It has low renal specificity and high proxy penalty.

Measurement count/frequency of low-flow oxygen documentation primarily reflects monitoring/documentation intensity rather than physiology. In the immediate post-op window it is especially process-driven and not renal-specific.
Frequency of recording low-flow oxygen use is mainly a clinician/monitoring proxy. It has weak and indirect association with AKI risk and should be down-weighted versus physiologic variables.

Documentation frequency in 25–36h may correlate with instability, but it remains primarily a monitoring-intensity signal. Per rules, _measured features are low value for mechanistic prediction of creatinine rise.

High-flow oxygen use early post-op suggests more significant respiratory support needs, which can correlate with shock/low cardiac output and AKI risk. However, in 0–12h it is confounded by routine post-op respiratory management and is still indirect.

Sustained high-flow oxygen requirement in 13–24h indicates ongoing cardiopulmonary dysfunction and higher illness severity, which is a moderate AKI risk context. Timing in the trajectory phase improves prognostic value, though renal specificity remains indirect.

Persistent high-flow oxygen in 25–36h is a closer-in-time marker of significant ongoing physiologic stress (e.g., pulmonary edema/low output) that can precede worsening renal function. Still a proxy rather than a direct renal biomarker, so not top-tier.

Any high-flow oxygen use in 0–12h flags early respiratory compromise, which may co-occur with hemodynamic instability relevant to AKI. Immediate post-op confounding and binary max aggregation limit specificity and predictive strength.

Any high-flow oxygen use in 13–24h suggests clinically meaningful respiratory failure/volume overload or low-output physiology, which is a moderate predictor of subsequent creatinine rise. Binary max captures an important extreme exposure better than mean for acute deterioration.

Represents need for high-flow oxygen support in the 25–36h window, a marker of respiratory failure/overall acuity. It is only indirectly related to AKI risk (hypoxemia/shock correlation) and is a care-intensity proxy rather than a renal-specific signal.

Indicates whether high-flow oxygen was used at all in the immediate 0–12h postoperative period. Early oxygen modality is often transient and heavily influenced by routine post-op practice, with weak specificity for subsequent creatinine rise.

Captures persistence of high-flow oxygen requirement into 13–24h, suggesting slower cardiopulmonary recovery. This has an indirect association with AKI via illness severity and possible hypoperfusion, but remains nonspecific.

Reflects ongoing need for high-flow oxygen in the closest pre-outcome window (25–36h), which can correlate with persistent low cardiac output or pulmonary edema. Still a nonspecific proxy and not a direct renal perfusion or injury marker.
Measurement/recording frequency of high-flow oxygen use in 0–12h mainly reflects documentation/monitoring intensity. Per rules, _measured variables are weak proxies and not mechanistically renal.
Measurement frequency of high-flow oxygen in 13–24h is primarily a care-process/monitoring signal. It adds little renal-specific information beyond the underlying oxygen device status.
Measurement frequency of high-flow oxygen in 25–36h is a monitoring-intensity proxy rather than physiology. Even near the outcome window, it is not a direct indicator of evolving AKI.
Recording frequency of first-degree AV block in 0–12h is largely documentation/telemetry artifact and common post-op. It has minimal direct linkage to renal perfusion or kidney injury.
Measurement frequency of first-degree AV block in 13–24h reflects monitoring and coding rather than a renal mechanism. Any association with AKI would be indirect and weak.
Measurement frequency of first-degree AV block in 25–36h is still a care-process proxy. First-degree block rarely drives hemodynamic compromise sufficient to predict creatinine ratio.

Measurement frequency of higher-grade AV block (2nd/3rd degree) in 0–12h may indicate clinically significant conduction issues and potential low cardiac output episodes. However, as a _measured variable it is still mostly a proxy and early post-op abnormalities can be transient.

Measurement frequency of 2nd/3rd degree AV block in 13–24h suggests ongoing conduction instability that could correlate with hypotension and renal hypoperfusion. Still penalized as a monitoring/documentation proxy rather than a direct physiologic extreme.

Measurement frequency of 2nd/3rd degree AV block in 25–36h is closer to the Hour-60 creatinine outcome and may reflect persistent hemodynamic risk. Nonetheless it remains a _measured proxy and not a direct renal biomarker.

Measurement frequency of bundle branch block in 0–12h is often baseline or perioperative conduction change and mainly reflects ECG documentation. It has little renal specificity and weak prognostic proximity to AKI.

Measurement frequency of bundle branch block in 13–24h is a monitoring/coding signal with limited physiologic granularity. Any relationship to creatinine rise would be indirect via overall cardiac disease burden.

Measurement frequency of bundle branch block in 25–36h remains a documentation proxy and does not quantify hemodynamic compromise. It is therefore unlikely to add meaningful predictive value for Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

Mean ionized calcium in the immediate postop period reflects transfusion/citrate load, CPB effects, and overall physiologic stress more than kidney-specific injury. Early mean values can wash out brief hypocalcemic episodes and are only indirectly linked to later creatinine rise.

Ionized calcium mean in the trajectory phase may reflect ongoing critical illness, transfusion, or endocrine/metabolic derangements rather than a direct AKI pathway. Still indirect for renal outcome and mean aggregation dilutes extremes.

Later ionized calcium mean is temporally closer to Hour 60 and may correlate with severity of illness or ongoing resuscitation, but it is not a direct renal biomarker. Mean values remain a relatively nonspecific proxy signal.

Peak ionized calcium early after surgery is usually driven by supplementation practices and lab timing rather than renal physiology. High values have weak mechanistic linkage to subsequent creatinine increase and carry a clinician-behavior proxy penalty.

Maximum ionized calcium in this window often reflects replacement strategy and monitoring rather than kidney perfusion or injury. Limited renal specificity and substantial treatment/measurement confounding.

Late peak ionized calcium is still more indicative of supplementation and metabolic management than evolving AKI. Despite better timing, it remains a weak and behavior-influenced predictor of creatinine ratio.

Early nadir ionized calcium can capture citrate-related hypocalcemia from transfusion/CPB and severe physiologic stress, which may co-occur with shock states relevant to AKI. However, in 0–12h it is often transient and not kidney-specific.
A low ionized calcium nadir in 13–24h may reflect ongoing critical illness and resuscitation needs that correlate with AKI risk. Still indirect and not a strong proximal renal mechanism on its own.

A low ionized calcium nadir closer to the outcome may signal persistent metabolic derangement, ongoing transfusion/citrate effect, or severe illness with hemodynamic instability that can precede AKI. Min aggregation captures clinically meaningful extremes better than mean/max here.
Measurement count mainly reflects monitoring intensity and clinician concern in the immediate postop period. It is a weak, nonspecific proxy with high behavior confounding.
Frequency of ionized calcium checks is primarily a care-process/acuity marker rather than a renal mechanism. Limited direct relevance to predicting Hour 60 creatinine ratio.

More frequent late calcium monitoring may indicate ongoing instability, transfusion, or electrolyte management complexity, which can correlate with AKI risk. Still largely a proxy for clinician behavior, so capped at low importance.

Mean magnesium early after cardiac surgery reflects supplementation, shifts from CPB, and general illness severity more than kidney injury. Renal relevance is indirect (reduced excretion can raise Mg), and early means may dilute important extremes.

Magnesium mean in 13–24h can weakly reflect renal clearance and ongoing replacement practices, but it is not a direct AKI biomarker. Moderate proxy penalty and mean aggregation reduce signal for acute insults.

Later magnesium mean is closer to Hour 60 and may begin to reflect impaired renal excretion or sustained supplementation needs in sicker patients. Still indirect, but temporal proximity makes it a more useful moderate predictor.

Early peak magnesium may reflect aggressive replacement or reduced clearance, but in the immediate postop window it is heavily influenced by clinician dosing and transient shifts. It has limited renal specificity yet may weakly track early renal dysfunction risk.

Peak magnesium in the 13–24h window mainly reflects supplementation/renal clearance but is not a direct AKI biomarker. Moderate temporal proximity but high proxy/iatrogenic influence keeps predictive value low.
Later peak magnesium may rise with reduced renal excretion, but in ICU it is often driven by replacement practices. Closer timing helps slightly, yet renal specificity remains weak so it stays low.

Early postoperative magnesium nadir is commonly due to hemodilution, shifts, and routine repletion rather than kidney injury. Immediate post-op transients and low renal specificity make it minimally useful.
Magnesium minimum in 13–24h more often reflects ongoing replacement strategy and intracellular shifts than renal dysfunction. It is an indirect, noisy signal for predicting creatinine rise.

A low magnesium nadir closer to outcome can correlate with diuretic use or poor nutrition and occasionally with renal handling, but it is still nonspecific. Timing is favorable but mechanism is indirect.
Measurement count is primarily monitoring intensity/clinical workflow rather than physiology. In the immediate post-op period it is routine and provides little renal-specific signal.
Frequency of magnesium checks mostly reflects clinician behavior and protocolized ICU care. It is not a renal mechanism and is only a weak proxy for instability.

More frequent magnesium measurement late may indicate ongoing electrolyte management in sicker patients (possible proxy for evolving organ dysfunction). Still a monitoring-intensity variable, so capped at low importance.

Mean base excess reflects overall metabolic acidosis/alkalosis from perfusion, lactate, and resuscitation, which can relate to renal hypoperfusion. Early post-op values are often transient and diluted by averaging.
Sustained metabolic acidosis in 13–24h suggests ongoing shock, poor perfusion, or high lactate burden, which increases AKI risk. Mean captures persistence reasonably well in this trajectory phase.

Persistently abnormal base excess close to Hour 60 is a strong indicator of ongoing metabolic derangement and inadequate perfusion, both linked to evolving AKI. The 25–36h mean reflects sustained exposure near the outcome.

Maximum base excess (most alkalemic) early after surgery often reflects hyperventilation, bicarbonate administration, or transfusion effects rather than renal injury. It has low renal specificity and high treatment confounding.
A high (alkalemic) base excess peak is usually driven by ventilation/alkali therapy and is not a typical AKI pathway signal. Prognostic proximity to creatinine rise is weak.
Late alkalemia can reflect aggressive diuresis/ventilation or bicarbonate use and is only indirectly related to renal outcomes. Closer timing adds some signal, but it remains treatment-confounded.

Minimum base excess captures worst metabolic acidosis early, which can indicate hypoperfusion and ischemic risk to kidneys. However, immediate post-op acidosis is common and may be transient, so not top-tier.

Worst base excess (most acidemic) in 13–24h is a strong marker of ongoing shock/poor perfusion and correlates with AKI risk. Using the minimum preserves clinically meaningful extremes better than the mean.

Most negative base excess reflects metabolic acidosis from hypoperfusion/lactate burden, which is a strong near-term correlate of shock-related AKI. The 25–36h minimum captures clinically meaningful extremes close to the Hour 60 creatinine outcome.

Measurement frequency of base excess mainly reflects monitoring intensity immediately post-op rather than renal physiology. Early post-bypass labs are routine and transient, so this is a weak proxy signal.
How often base excess is checked is largely clinician/acuity driven and not a direct kidney injury marker. Slightly more informative than 0–12h but still primarily a process-of-care proxy.
Frequent base excess checks in the 25–36h window can indicate ongoing instability/shock workup, which can correlate with AKI risk. Still predominantly a monitoring-intensity proxy, so capped low.

Direct bilirubin measurement frequency reflects lab ordering/concern for hepatic dysfunction rather than kidney-specific injury. In the immediate post-op period it is often routine and not tightly linked to creatinine rise.
This is primarily a clinician behavior/monitoring signal for cholestasis or hepatic injury, not a renal mechanism. Any association with AKI is indirect via overall illness severity.
More frequent direct bilirubin checks later may mark persistent organ dysfunction or low-flow state, which can co-occur with AKI. However it remains a process proxy and not kidney-specific.
Mean WBC reflects postoperative inflammatory response/stress, which is nonspecific and common after cardiac surgery. Early averages can wash out peaks and are only indirectly related to AKI risk.

Sustained leukocytosis in the trajectory phase may indicate ongoing inflammation/infection risk, which can contribute to AKI indirectly. Mean aggregation is still nonspecific and less sensitive than extremes.
Persistently elevated mean WBC closer to the outcome can signal evolving infection/SIRS, a moderate AKI risk factor in ICU patients. Still indirect and nonspecific to renal injury, so not top-tier.
Peak WBC early post-op captures the inflammatory surge but is often transient and not kidney-specific. Extremes are better than mean, yet timing is less predictive for Hour 60 creatinine.
A high WBC peak in 13–24h may reflect infection/SIRS or ongoing stress, which is a moderate predictor of subsequent AKI. Max aggregation captures clinically relevant extremes better than mean.

A late peak WBC near 25–36h can indicate evolving infection/inflammation, which can worsen renal function via sepsis physiology and nephrotoxic exposures. Still an indirect marker rather than a renal biomarker.
Low WBC early is uncommon and often reflects dilutional effects or lab variability rather than a renal pathway. It is nonspecific and temporally distant from the Hour 60 creatinine ratio.
WBC minimum is generally less informative than maximum for inflammatory risk and is not kidney-specific. Any association with AKI is weak and indirect.

A low WBC later could reflect severe illness/immunosuppression or dilution, which may correlate with complications including AKI, but the link is weak. Minimum aggregation is less pathophysiologically aligned than peak leukocytosis.

Frequency of WBC checks mainly reflects monitoring intensity/clinical concern rather than a renal mechanism. Early postop measurement counts are especially nonspecific for predicting Hour-60 creatinine.

WBC measurement frequency is a care-process proxy (infection/instability concern) with weak direct linkage to AKI biology. Trajectory window adds little because it is still not a physiologic value and is highly confounded by practice patterns.

Later measurement frequency may weakly signal evolving complications (sepsis/shock) that can precipitate AKI, but it remains primarily a clinician-behavior proxy. Per rules, _measured variables should stay low despite closer timing.

Mean PT reflects coagulation status (liver dysfunction, dilutional coagulopathy, anticoagulation) and can correlate with severity/bleeding/transfusion that indirectly affect renal perfusion. In the immediate postop window, abnormalities are common/transient and the mean can wash out short severe derangements.

Persistently prolonged PT by 25–36h suggests ongoing hepatic dysfunction, inflammation, or consumptive/dilutional coagulopathy and is a stronger marker of sustained critical illness that can accompany AKI. Mean is still less sensitive than extremes but timing is closer to the creatinine outcome.

Peak PT captures the worst coagulopathy early after surgery (often driven by bypass/hemodilution/anticoagulation reversal) and may relate indirectly to bleeding/transfusion and renal hypoperfusion. However, early peaks are frequently transient and not kidney-specific.

A high PT maximum in the 25–36h window indicates significant ongoing coagulopathy/organ dysfunction and can track with shock, transfusion needs, and systemic illness that worsen renal function. The extreme is more informative than the mean for capturing clinically meaningful derangements.
Low/normal PT minima are not a plausible driver of AKI and mostly reflect periods without coagulopathy. The minimum is a poor aggregation for capturing pathology relevant to renal injury.
PT minimum mainly reflects the best value achieved and does not encode renal hypoperfusion or kidney injury pathways. Even close to outcome, minima are typically uninformative compared with maxima.

PT measurement frequency is largely a monitoring/transfusion/anticoagulation-management proxy rather than a physiologic renal signal. Early postop testing is routine after cardiac surgery, limiting predictive specificity.

Ongoing PT check frequency reflects clinician concern for bleeding/coagulopathy and practice patterns more than kidney pathophysiology. As a _measured feature it carries high proxy penalty and low renal specificity.

Later frequent PT monitoring may weakly indicate persistent instability/bleeding risk that can co-occur with AKI, but it remains a care-process proxy. Closer timing modestly increases usefulness while still capped low for _measured variables.

NRBCs suggest severe physiologic stress/hypoxemia/inflammation and correlate with critical illness severity, which can be associated with AKI risk. In the immediate postop period this is less specific and may reflect transient peri-bypass stress.

Persistent NRBCs in the 13–24h trajectory phase can indicate ongoing hypoxia/shock/systemic stress, which is more prognostically meaningful for subsequent renal dysfunction. Mean is acceptable here because sustained presence (not just a spike) is clinically relevant.

NRBCs persisting into 25–36h are a strong marker of ongoing critical illness and hypoperfusion/inflammation that can precede worsening creatinine. Still indirect (not kidney-specific), so it does not reach top-tier scores.

Peak NRBCs early may capture a severe immediate postoperative stress response and correlate with hypoxia/shock that can contribute to AKI. However, early extremes can be transient and are not specific to renal injury mechanisms.

Peak NRBCs in 13–24h is a nonspecific marker of physiologic stress/hypoxemia/inflammation after surgery. It may correlate with overall severity and hypoperfusion but is indirect for kidney injury and prone to proxy/acuity confounding.

Peak NRBCs in 25–36h can reflect ongoing hypoxia/shock or marrow stress closer to the outcome window. Still a weak, nonspecific proxy rather than a renal-specific biomarker, so predictive value for creatinine rise is limited.

Minimum NRBCs in the immediate 0–12h period is unlikely to capture a renal-relevant insult and is often near zero. Early postoperative values are noisy and largely reflect general stress rather than AKI mechanisms.
Minimum NRBCs over 13–24h is not clinically meaningful for renal perfusion or evolving AKI compared with peaks or trends. It is largely redundant and nonspecific.
Minimum NRBCs in 25–36h does not represent a pathologic extreme relevant to kidney injury and is typically uninformative. Any association would be indirect and dominated by overall illness severity.
NRBC 'measured' mainly reflects lab ordering/monitoring intensity in the immediate postoperative period. This is a care-process proxy with minimal direct linkage to renal function at Hour 60.
Measurement frequency indicates clinician attention or instability rather than a kidney mechanism. At best it weakly tracks severity of illness, so it has low value for predicting creatinine_ratio.
More frequent NRBC checks in 25–36h may signal ongoing concern for hypoxia/hemolysis/severe illness closer to the outcome. Still primarily a monitoring-intensity proxy, so it should not score high.

Mean glucose in 0–12h reflects stress hyperglycemia and perioperative insulin management, which are indirect AKI risk correlates. Early postoperative means can wash out extremes and are confounded by treatment.

Mean glucose in 13–24h captures sustained dysglycemia during the trajectory phase, which may relate to inflammation and complications. Renal specificity is low and clinician-driven insulin titration adds proxy noise.

Mean glucose in 25–36h is closer to the Hour 60 creatinine and may reflect persistent stress response/infection or poor metabolic control associated with AKI risk. Still indirect for renal injury, but timing improves prognostic usefulness.

Peak glucose in 0–12h captures acute stress hyperglycemia, which can correlate with severity and adverse outcomes. However it is early, often transient, and heavily influenced by perioperative management, limiting renal-specific prediction.

Peak glucose in 13–24h may indicate ongoing stress/infection or inadequate control and is a stronger signal than the mean for acute derangements. It remains an indirect AKI predictor but has moderate prognostic value.

Peak glucose in 25–36h is a near-term marker of persistent physiologic stress and complications that can precede AKI. It is not kidney-specific, but the extreme and proximity to outcome make it a moderate predictor.

Minimum glucose in 0–12h can reflect hypoglycemia from insulin therapy or poor intake, which may associate with instability. It is largely treatment-driven and not renal-specific, so predictive value is limited.

Minimum glucose in 13–24h may capture hypoglycemic episodes indicating aggressive insulin use or critical illness. This is an indirect, clinician-influenced signal with weak mechanistic linkage to creatinine rise.

Minimum glucose in the 25–36h window reflects hypoglycemia/tight control or poor intake, which is only indirectly related to AKI risk. It may proxy overall instability but is not kidney-specific, so predictive value for creatinine_ratio is limited.

Frequency of glucose checks in the immediate postop period mainly reflects monitoring intensity/insulin protocol rather than renal physiology. Strong proxy penalty and early timing make it weak for predicting Hour-60 creatinine ratio.
Glucose measurement count is largely a care-process signal (insulin use/ICU protocol) rather than a mechanistic AKI marker. Even in the trajectory phase it remains nonspecific for renal outcomes.

More frequent glucose checks at 25–36h can indicate ongoing critical illness or insulin infusion, which may correlate weakly with AKI risk. Still primarily a monitoring-intensity proxy, so it should not score high.

Mean platelet count early after cardiac surgery reflects hemodilution/consumption from CPB and bleeding risk, which are indirect correlates of AKI. Early postoperative values are often transient, reducing specificity for Hour-60 creatinine_ratio.

Average platelets in 13–24h may reflect ongoing inflammation/consumption and overall severity, which can associate with AKI but indirectly. Mean aggregation can wash out clinically important nadirs, limiting predictive strength.

Persistently low mean platelets at 25–36h can signal sustained inflammatory/consumptive physiology or complications that track with AKI risk. Closer timing improves prognostic proximity, though it remains nonspecific to kidney injury.

Maximum platelet count in the immediate postop window is rarely pathologic and is not a recognized AKI pathway marker. It is likely noise or reflects transfusion/hemodilution dynamics with minimal renal specificity.
A high platelet maximum is generally not informative for renal perfusion or evolving AKI. It adds little beyond other platelet summaries and is largely non-mechanistic for creatinine_ratio.
Platelet maxima near 25–36h are typically not clinically meaningful for AKI prediction compared with thrombocytopenia/nadir measures. Low renal specificity and redundancy justify a minimal score.

Early platelet nadir can reflect CPB-related consumption, bleeding, or transfusion needs, which correlate with postoperative complications including AKI. Min captures the relevant extreme better than mean, but early timing warrants some discount.

Platelet nadir in 13–24h may indicate ongoing consumption/inflammation or complications that are moderately associated with AKI. The minimum is the most informative platelet aggregation for risk, though still indirect for kidney injury.

A low platelet nadir at 25–36h suggests sustained consumptive/inflammatory physiology or complications (e.g., sepsis/bleeding/low-flow states) that strongly track with subsequent AKI. This window is closest to Hour 60 and the min captures the clinically relevant extreme.

Platelet measurement frequency mainly reflects lab-ordering intensity and postoperative protocol rather than renal pathophysiology. Strong proxy penalty and early timing make it minimally useful for creatinine_ratio.
Count of platelet measurements is a care-process variable and not a direct marker of renal injury or perfusion. It is unlikely to add meaningful independent signal in a sparse linear model.

More frequent platelet checks at 25–36h may weakly indicate clinician concern for bleeding/consumption and higher illness severity, which can correlate with AKI. However it remains primarily a monitoring proxy and should stay low.

Mean corpuscular hemoglobin (MCH) reflects RBC indices/anemia characteristics rather than renal function or perfusion. Early postoperative mean values are nonspecific and unlikely to add direct signal for Hour-60 creatinine change.
MCH is a hematologic index with weak mechanistic linkage to AKI compared with hemodynamics, urine output, or renal labs. The 13–24h mean remains largely nonspecific for predicting creatinine_ratio.

Even close to the outcome window, MCH mainly reflects anemia/macrocytosis patterns and transfusion mix rather than evolving kidney injury. Any association would be indirect and redundant with more proximal perfusion/renal markers.

Peak MCH in the immediate postoperative period is not a recognized AKI pathway marker and is often driven by lab variability or transfusion-related shifts. Extremes here do not meaningfully capture renal risk.
Maximum MCH over 13–24h remains a weak, indirect proxy (anemia indices/transfusion effects) with low renal specificity. It is unlikely to improve sparse prediction of creatinine_ratio.
Although temporally closer, a high MCH is not a direct biomarker of renal injury or hypoperfusion. Any signal would be incidental and dominated by more kidney-relevant labs and hemodynamics.

Minimum MCH early after surgery is nonspecific and does not reflect renal perfusion or tubular injury. Low values are more related to baseline hematology or dilution/transfusion patterns than AKI risk.

Low MCH in this window is still primarily a hematologic characteristic and not an established near-term precursor of postoperative creatinine rise. It offers minimal renal-specific prognostic information.

Minimum MCH close to Hour 60 remains an indirect, low-specificity marker and is unlikely to track evolving AKI. It is also likely redundant with hemoglobin/hematocrit and transfusion variables if present.
The count/frequency of MCH measurements mainly reflects CBC ordering/monitoring intensity rather than physiology. As a process-of-care proxy, it has minimal direct relevance to creatinine_ratio.

Measurement frequency is largely clinician behavior and correlates with general acuity rather than kidney-specific mechanisms. Per rules, _measured variables are low value for predicting renal outcomes.
Even in the leading-indicator window, how often MCH is measured is a weak proxy for monitoring intensity and not a renal biomarker. It should not be prioritized in a sparse renal prediction model.

Mean serum sodium reflects fluid balance, dilution, and neurohormonal state; it can correlate with volume status and illness severity but is not kidney-specific. In the immediate postoperative period, sodium is often influenced by resuscitation and bypass-related shifts, limiting specificity.

Sodium over 13–24h may better reflect evolving free-water balance/diuresis and can indirectly relate to renal handling and volume management. Still, it is a nonspecific marker and not a direct AKI biomarker.

By 25–36h, persistent dysnatremia can reflect ongoing fluid/diuretic response and impaired renal water handling, making it a more informative leading indicator. Mean sodium captures sustained exposure but remains indirect compared with creatinine/urine output/hemodynamics.

Peak sodium early may indicate hypertonic exposure, hemoconcentration, or free-water deficit, which can accompany hypoperfusion/diuresis but is still indirect for AKI. The 0–12h timing is prone to transient peri-bypass/resuscitation effects, so predictive value is limited.

Peak serum sodium in the 13–24h window reflects fluid/solute balance and hypernatremia from diuresis or free-water deficit. It is only indirectly related to AKI risk and is a nonspecific illness/management marker.
Peak sodium closer to the outcome may capture evolving water balance issues or aggressive diuresis. Still a weak, indirect proxy for renal dysfunction rather than a kidney-specific biomarker.

Early postoperative nadir sodium can reflect hemodilution, hypotonic fluids, or SIADH-like physiology after surgery. Immediate post-op abnormalities are common/transient and only weakly predictive of later creatinine rise.
Nadir sodium in 13–24h may indicate ongoing dilutional states or excess free water administration. Association with AKI is indirect and confounded by resuscitation and care processes.
Later nadir sodium could reflect persistent fluid overload or impaired water handling, which can co-occur with AKI. However sodium is not specific for renal injury and remains a modest proxy signal.

Count/indicator of sodium measurements mainly reflects monitoring intensity and clinician concern in the immediate post-op period. It does not represent a renal mechanism and is highly process-driven.
Frequency of sodium checks in 13–24h is largely a care/acuity proxy (e.g., electrolyte replacement protocols). It has minimal direct linkage to kidney injury pathways.
More frequent sodium measurement late (25–36h) may signal instability, diuretic titration, or evolving renal/volume issues. Still primarily a monitoring-intensity proxy, so capped at low importance.

Whether TEG platelet aggregation was measured reflects bleeding/thrombosis evaluation and transfusion planning rather than renal physiology. Any association with creatinine is indirect via overall surgical complexity.
TEG platelet aggregation measurement in 13–24h is a clinician-behavior marker for coagulopathy/bleeding management. It has no specific mechanistic tie to AKI and is mostly a proxy.
Late TEG platelet aggregation measurement indicates ongoing hemostasis concerns or anticoagulation management. It remains nonspecific for renal injury and is dominated by process/indication bias.

Mean MCHC reflects red cell hemoglobin concentration and is influenced by transfusion/hemodilution and lab artifact. It has little renal specificity and early post-op values are unlikely to predict Hour-60 creatinine ratio.
Average MCHC in 13–24h is a nonspecific hematologic index with weak connection to renal perfusion or injury. Any signal would be indirect through anemia/transfusion patterns.
Later mean MCHC remains a low-specificity RBC index and does not capture a known AKI pathway. Temporal proximity does not overcome the lack of renal relevance.

Maximum MCHC early post-op is often driven by measurement variability, hemoconcentration, or transfusion effects. It is not a meaningful renal biomarker and is unlikely to add predictive value for creatinine rise.
Peak MCHC in 13–24h is still a nonspecific hematology extreme without established prognostic proximity to AKI. It mainly reflects lab/volume status noise rather than kidney injury.

Peak MCHC in the 25–36h window reflects RBC indices/hemoconcentration or lab artifact rather than renal function. Only weakly related to AKI via indirect anemia/volume status signals, so limited specificity for creatinine rise.

Early postoperative low MCHC is a nonspecific RBC index influenced by hemodilution/transfusion and peri-bypass effects. It has minimal direct linkage to renal injury and is temporally distant with high noise.

Low MCHC in 13–24h remains a nonspecific hematologic index and does not directly reflect renal perfusion or injury. Any association with creatinine is indirect and likely redundant with better anemia/oxygen delivery measures.

Later low MCHC may correlate with anemia/hemodilution near the outcome window but is still not kidney-specific. At best it is an indirect proxy for oxygen delivery/bleeding/transfusion rather than a mechanistic AKI marker.

Measurement count mainly reflects lab ordering intensity in the immediate postoperative period. This is a care-process proxy with low renal specificity and should not strongly predict Hour-60 creatinine ratio.
Frequency of MCHC measurement is primarily monitoring intensity and clinician behavior rather than physiology. It has weak and nonspecific association with evolving AKI.
More frequent labs in 25–36h can signal instability or concern (including renal), but it remains a proxy variable. Per rules, measurement features are capped low and are not kidney-mechanistic.

Mean hematocrit early after surgery reflects hemodilution, bleeding, and transfusion, which can affect oxygen delivery but is not kidney-specific. Immediate postoperative values are often transient, limiting prognostic proximity for Hour-60 creatinine.

Mean hematocrit in 13–24h may capture ongoing anemia/hemodilution that can contribute to renal hypoxia, but the link to AKI is indirect. Mean aggregation can wash out short critical lows relevant to renal perfusion.

Sustained anemia/hemodilution closer to the outcome window can plausibly worsen renal oxygen delivery and correlate with ongoing bleeding/transfusion burden. Still indirect for AKI, but timing improves usefulness.

Early peak hematocrit is usually driven by hemoconcentration or sampling variability and is not a recognized AKI pathway marker. It is temporally distant and less informative than hypotension/oliguria/creatinine trends.
Peak hematocrit in 13–24h is a weak, nonspecific signal and not kidney-specific. It is generally less relevant than nadir hematocrit for oxygen delivery-related risk.

Later peak hematocrit may reflect hemoconcentration from diuresis/negative fluid balance, which can relate to perfusion/volume status but remains indirect. It is less mechanistically tied to AKI than hypotension or low urine output.

Early nadir hematocrit captures acute anemia/hemodilution/bleeding, which can impair oxygen delivery and contribute to renal ischemic risk. Despite early timing discount, the extreme (min) is the most clinically relevant aggregation in this family.

Nadir hematocrit in 13–24h reflects persistent or worsening anemia and transfusion/bleeding burden, which can be associated with AKI risk via renal hypoxia and inflammation. Extreme aggregation is appropriate and more informative than the mean.

Low hematocrit closest to Hour 60 is a strong near-term indicator of inadequate oxygen delivery and ongoing bleeding/transfusion physiology that can precipitate or accompany AKI. The minimum captures clinically meaningful extremes and is the best-performing aggregation among hematocrit features.

Count/frequency of hematocrit checks in the immediate postop window mainly reflects monitoring intensity and transfusion/bleeding concern rather than renal physiology. As a _measured proxy and early epoch, it has minimal direct value for predicting Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

Measurement frequency of hematocrit during 13–24h is largely a care-process/acuity signal (bleeding/anemia surveillance) with weak mechanistic linkage to AKI. _measured variables are penalized and should not be high-importance for creatinine trajectory.

Later hematocrit monitoring frequency may correlate with ongoing instability or transfusion needs, which can indirectly relate to renal hypoperfusion risk. Still, it is primarily clinician-behavior/monitoring intensity rather than a renal-specific marker.

Basophil count is a nonspecific hematologic differential component with no established direct pathway to postoperative AKI. The early mean value is unlikely to add meaningful signal for Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

Mean basophil count in 13–24h remains a nonspecific inflammatory/allergic marker with weak renal specificity and poor prognostic proximity to AKI. Any association would be indirect and likely redundant with broader illness markers.

A peak basophil count shortly after surgery is rarely clinically meaningful for renal perfusion or tubular injury and is often noise. Despite using an extreme, it lacks renal specificity and temporal relevance to creatinine rise.

Maximum basophil count in 13–24h does not capture a known AKI mechanism and is unlikely to be a stable predictor in sparse linear models. Any signal would be indirect and low-yield compared with hemodynamics, urine output, or renal labs.

Minimum basophil count in the immediate postop period is not a recognized indicator of renal injury or perfusion failure. The aggregation is unlikely to reflect relevant pathology for creatinine ratio at 60h.
Low basophil counts in 13–24h are nonspecific and not mechanistically tied to AKI development. Predictive utility for Hour-60 creatinine ratio is expected to be minimal.
Frequency of basophil measurement is essentially frequency of CBC/differential ordering, a monitoring-intensity proxy. It has little renal specificity and is heavily penalized as a _measured feature.

CBC/differential measurement frequency in 13–24h reflects clinician surveillance and general acuity rather than kidney injury pathways. As a _measured proxy, it offers minimal direct predictive value for creatinine ratio.

Later CBC/differential measurement frequency may weakly track ongoing complications (infection/bleeding) that can secondarily affect renal function. However, it remains a monitoring-intensity proxy and not a renal biomarker.

Mean lymphocyte percentage early after surgery reflects stress response/inflammation and overall physiologic stress, which can correlate indirectly with AKI risk. The 0–12h mean is discounted because transient postoperative leukocyte shifts are common and nonspecific.

Mean lymphocyte percentage in 13–24h may better reflect evolving inflammation or infection risk than the immediate postop period, with a weak indirect link to AKI. Still, it is not renal-specific and is a modest predictor at best.

A high lymphocyte percentage in the immediate postop window is generally nonspecific and may reflect relative neutropenia/hemodilution rather than a renal mechanism. Using a max does not strongly improve renal relevance in this early epoch.

Peak lymphocyte percentage in 13–24h could weakly signal atypical inflammatory trajectories, but it remains an indirect and nonspecific marker for AKI. Predictive value for Hour-60 creatinine ratio is likely limited compared with renal/perfusion variables.
Minimum lymphocyte percentage early post-op mainly reflects stress response/inflammation or dilution rather than renal function. It is nonspecific for AKI and the immediate 0–12h window is noisy.

Low lymphocyte percentage in 13–24h may indicate ongoing stress/inflammation but has weak mechanistic linkage to renal injury. It is an indirect severity marker with limited specificity for predicting creatinine rise.
Whether lymphocyte percent was measured is primarily a monitoring-intensity/lab-ordering proxy. It does not encode kidney physiology and should be heavily penalized as a process-of-care signal.
Measurement presence reflects clinician behavior and overall acuity rather than a renal pathway. Not a direct or reliable predictor of Hour-60 creatinine ratio.
Even in the closer 25–36h window, 'measured' is still a process proxy and not a physiologic renal signal. Monitoring frequency alone should not be weighted for AKI prediction.

Having a random vancomycin level measured suggests vancomycin exposure and/or concern for drug accumulation, which can relate to renal function. However it is still largely a clinician-ordering proxy and early timing limits specificity.

Random vancomycin level measurement may indicate ongoing vancomycin therapy with potential nephrotoxic risk or emerging renal dysfunction. Still indirect (measured vs level) and confounded by practice patterns.

In the 25–36h leading window, ordering a random vanc level can signal concern for impaired clearance near the outcome time. Yet it remains a monitoring proxy rather than a direct biomarker, so it should stay low-moderate.

Trough measurement implies vancomycin exposure and attention to dosing/clearance, which can correlate with renal reserve. But it is a process variable and early post-op ordering is not tightly linked to subsequent creatinine ratio.

Trough measurement in 13–24h may reflect ongoing therapy and concern for accumulation as renal function evolves. Still indirect and heavily influenced by institutional protocols rather than kidney injury itself.

Trough measurement closer to Hour 60 can be a weak signal of suspected reduced clearance or nephrotoxin exposure. However, as a 'measured' indicator it is confounded by clinician behavior and not a direct renal marker.

Mean oxygen saturation reflects oxygenation/respiratory status and global illness severity, which can affect renal perfusion indirectly. In 0–12h it is often transiently influenced by anesthesia/ventilation, reducing prognostic value for AKI.

Sustained lower mean SpO2 in 13–24h may indicate ongoing hypoxemia and systemic stress that can contribute to renal injury indirectly. Still nonspecific and not a direct renal perfusion or kidney function measure.

Mean SpO2 in 25–36h captures more sustained hypoxemia closer to the outcome window, which can worsen renal oxygen delivery and correlate with evolving organ dysfunction. It remains indirect but has better temporal proximity than earlier epochs.

Maximum SpO2 is usually near 100% and reflects supplemental oxygen/ventilator settings more than physiology relevant to AKI. As an extreme in the 'good' direction, it has little renal prognostic content.

Max SpO2 largely captures ability to achieve high saturation with support and is typically non-informative for renal outcomes. It is a weak, treatment-dependent metric with minimal linkage to creatinine rise.
Peak SpO2 in the 25–36h window mainly reflects adequate oxygenation and is rarely limiting for renal perfusion. Max values are typically normal/saturated and add little AKI-specific signal.

Early postoperative hypoxemia can contribute to systemic stress and renal ischemia, but 0–12h abnormalities are often transient from anesthesia/ventilation changes. The minimum captures extremes, yet renal specificity is indirect.

Persistent or recurrent hypoxemia in 13–24h may indicate ongoing cardiopulmonary dysfunction that can worsen renal oxygen delivery. Still an indirect proxy for AKI risk rather than a kidney-specific marker.

Low SpO2 closest to the outcome window can signal sustained respiratory/cardiac failure and reduced renal oxygen delivery, which is more prognostically relevant for later creatinine rise. Minimum is the right aggregation for capturing clinically meaningful hypoxemic insults.
Measurement count/frequency is primarily a monitoring-intensity signal rather than physiology. It is weakly related to renal outcomes and heavily confounded by nursing/ICU workflow.
SpO2 measurement frequency mostly reflects care processes and perceived instability, not a renal mechanism. Proxy penalty is high and it is not a direct predictor of creatinine trajectory.

More frequent SpO2 checks in 25–36h may indicate clinical concern or instability near the outcome window, which can correlate with AKI risk. However it remains a behavior/monitoring proxy, so it should not score high.

ALT reflects hepatocellular injury and can rise with perioperative hypoperfusion (‘shock liver’), indirectly signaling systemic low-flow states relevant to AKI. Mean in 0–12h may dilute brief ischemic spikes and is temporally early.

Peak ALT early after surgery can indicate ischemic hepatitis from severe hypotension/low cardiac output, which is a plausible AKI context. It is still indirect for kidney injury and early postoperative noise limits specificity.
Minimum ALT is not clinically meaningful for hypoperfusion or organ injury and is often just baseline/assay variation. It has little mechanistic linkage to subsequent creatinine rise.
ALT measurement frequency mainly reflects ordering behavior and monitoring intensity rather than hepatic or renal physiology. High proxy penalty and weak renal specificity.
How often ALT is checked is largely clinician-driven and nonspecific, correlating more with general acuity than kidney injury pathways. Not a strong predictor of creatinine_ratio.
ALT measurement frequency near 25–36h remains a care-process proxy and does not directly encode renal function or perfusion. Proxy penalty outweighs any indirect association.

BUN is a kidney-related marker influenced by renal perfusion, catabolism, and volume status, and can move earlier than creatinine in prerenal states. In 0–12h it is somewhat informative but can be confounded by hemodilution/transfusion and early postoperative shifts.

Mean BUN in 13–24h reflects evolving renal clearance and prerenal physiology during the trajectory phase. It is moderately predictive but less specific than creatinine and affected by nutrition/catabolism.

BUN in 25–36h is temporally close to the Hour 60 creatinine ratio and reflects reduced renal clearance and/or sustained prerenal azotemia. This window provides a strong leading indicator of impending creatinine rise despite some nonrenal confounding.

Peak BUN early post-op reflects renal clearance plus catabolism/volume status; it is kidney-relevant but can be confounded by hemodilution/resuscitation immediately after surgery. Max captures potential early azotemia, but timing is somewhat premature for Hour-60 creatinine rise.
Peak BUN in the trajectory phase can signal evolving azotemia from reduced GFR or hypoperfusion. Still moderately indirect vs creatinine and influenced by nonrenal factors, so not top-tier.

Late-window peak BUN is closer to the Hour-60 outcome and can reflect worsening renal clearance or sustained hypoperfusion. Max aggregation captures clinically meaningful azotemia and is a strong near-term correlate of rising creatinine.

Minimum BUN early post-op often reflects dilution from fluids/CPB rather than renal protection, making it a weak and potentially misleading renal signal. Min is less aligned with AKI pathophysiology than peak azotemia.
Minimum BUN in this window is more about volume status/hemodilution and nutrition than kidney injury. It is renal-adjacent but not a strong predictor of subsequent creatinine ratio.

Even close to the outcome, a low BUN is nonspecific and may reflect dilution or low protein intake rather than preserved GFR. Min aggregation does not capture the insult most relevant to AKI prediction.
Whether/how often BUN was measured is primarily a monitoring-intensity/clinician-behavior proxy. It has minimal direct mechanistic linkage to Hour-60 creatinine ratio.
BUN measurement frequency mainly reflects perceived acuity or protocolized labs rather than renal physiology. Proxy penalty is high, so predictive value should be low.
Late-window BUN measurement may indicate clinician concern for evolving AKI closer to the outcome, giving a small signal. Still largely a care-process proxy, so capped low.
Amylase measurement indicates evaluation for pancreatitis/ischemia or nonspecific abdominal concerns, not kidney function. Any association with creatinine is indirect via overall illness severity.
Amylase being checked is a clinician-behavior marker with little renal specificity. It is unlikely to add meaningful signal for Hour-60 creatinine ratio beyond general acuity.
Even near the outcome window, amylase measurement is not mechanistically tied to AKI and mainly reflects diagnostic workup intensity. Proxy penalty dominates.

Mean pH early post-op reflects acid-base status from CPB, ventilation, and lactate; severe acidosis can accompany hypoperfusion that risks AKI. Mean in the immediate period can wash out brief insults and is often transient.

Persistently low mean pH in this phase suggests ongoing shock/lactic acidosis or renal acid handling impairment, which is more prognostically relevant for AKI. Mean captures sustained derangement reasonably well here.

Late-window mean pH close to Hour 60 can reflect ongoing hypoperfusion or accumulating metabolic acidosis from declining renal function. It is a moderate near-term physiologic correlate but still indirect vs direct renal biomarkers.

Maximum pH (alkalemia) early post-op is usually driven by ventilation/bicarbonate administration and is not a typical AKI pathway signal. As an extreme in the non-pathologic direction for renal injury, it has low relevance.

Represents peak alkalemia in the 13–24h trajectory phase; pH is a nonspecific marker of ventilation/acid-base management rather than kidney injury. Max pH is weakly related to renal outcomes and is often a care/respiratory proxy.

Peak alkalemia closer to the outcome window may reflect ongoing ventilation strategy or diuretic/contraction alkalosis, but remains indirect for AKI. Temporal proximity helps slightly, yet renal specificity is low.

Lowest pH early post-op captures acidemia from hypoperfusion/lactate, which can precede AKI via shock pathways. However, immediate post-bypass derangements are common/transient, so predictive value is moderated.

Nadir pH in 13–24h reflects persistent or recurrent acidemia (often from ongoing hypoperfusion/sepsis/respiratory failure), which is a plausible AKI precursor. Still indirect and influenced by ventilation and buffering, so not top-tier.

Nadir pH in 25–36h is a near-term signal of ongoing systemic hypoperfusion/critical illness that can drive evolving AKI and higher creatinine at 60h. The extreme (min) captures clinically meaningful insults better than mean/max.

Indicates frequency/presence of pH measurement in the immediate post-op period, mainly reflecting monitoring intensity and clinician concern. It is not a physiologic renal mechanism and is heavily confounded by routine post-cardiac surgery protocols.
Measurement indicator is primarily a care-process proxy (acuity/ventilation management) rather than kidney-specific information. Limited incremental value for predicting creatinine ratio.
More frequent pH checks late (25–36h) may signal instability prompting closer monitoring, which can correlate with AKI risk. Still largely a proxy variable, so capped low.
Cholesterol measurement is unrelated to acute renal perfusion or kidney injury pathways and mostly reflects baseline risk assessment or routine labs. Minimal relevance to 60h creatinine change.
A monitoring/ordering artifact with little mechanistic link to postoperative AKI. Unlikely to add predictive signal beyond comorbidity context.
Late cholesterol measurement remains a nonspecific care-process variable without renal specificity. Very low expected utility for predicting creatinine ratio.
Mean lymphocyte count early post-op reflects stress response/inflammation and overall illness severity, which can correlate with AKI risk indirectly. Early timing and nonspecificity limit strength.

Trajectory-phase lymphopenia can mark ongoing systemic inflammation or physiologic stress associated with AKI, but it is not kidney-specific. Mean aggregation may dilute clinically important extremes.
Peak lymphocyte count in the immediate post-op window is not a recognized AKI precursor and is highly nonspecific. Max is unlikely to capture relevant pathology compared with low counts.
Higher lymphocyte values are generally not informative for AKI risk and mainly reflect nonspecific hematologic variation. Limited renal relevance and weak prognostic proximity.

Early nadir lymphocyte count captures stress lymphopenia, which can correlate with severity of illness and inflammatory burden linked to AKI. However, it is indirect and early post-op effects are common, so only modest importance.

Minimum lymphocyte count reflects stress/inflammation and overall illness severity rather than a renal-specific pathway. Mid-epoch timing is only moderately informative and the signal is indirect for predicting creatinine rise.
Measurement indicator mainly captures monitoring intensity/ordering behavior, not kidney physiology. Early postoperative period further limits specificity for Hour 60 creatinine trajectory.
This is primarily a care-process/monitoring proxy rather than a mechanistic renal predictor. Even in the trajectory phase, frequency of checking lymphocytes is weakly tied to AKI risk.
Still a monitoring-intensity proxy, but closer timing may reflect clinician concern in a deteriorating patient approaching the outcome window. Not renal-specific, so it remains low importance.

Glucose measurement presence is largely protocol-driven in post-cardiac surgery ICU care and reflects monitoring rather than renal injury. Early epoch and proxy nature make it minimally useful for creatinine_ratio.
Binary indicator of glucose being checked is a weak proxy for acuity/insulin use and not kidney-specific. It adds little direct information about evolving AKI.

Closer to Hour 60, more frequent glucose checks may correlate with instability or high-dose insulin/vasopressors, indirectly associated with AKI risk. Still primarily a monitoring proxy, so capped low.

CK measurement presence reflects clinician suspicion for muscle injury/ischemia rather than renal function itself. In the immediate postoperative window it is especially nonspecific and mostly a process marker.

Ordering CK may indicate concern for rhabdomyolysis/ischemia, which can contribute to AKI, but this variable is only a proxy for that concern. Mid-epoch timing gives limited incremental prediction without the CK value.
Nearer to the outcome, CK being checked may signal ongoing tissue injury with potential renal consequences, but it remains a clinician-behavior proxy. Not a direct renal biomarker, so low importance.

Mean eGFR is a direct kidney function estimate and strongly relates to renal reserve and early postoperative dysfunction. However, immediate post-op creatinine/eGFR can be transiently distorted by dilution and hemodynamics, so slightly discounted.

eGFR in the 13–24h trajectory phase reflects recovery vs evolving AKI and is renal-specific. Mean aggregation captures sustained dysfunction reasonably well, though it may miss brief insults compared with extremes.
This is a direct renal function marker in the closest pre-outcome window, making it highly prognostic for Hour 60 creatinine_ratio. Sustained reduction in eGFR here strongly signals evolving AKI.

Maximum eGFR largely reflects the best (often dilutional) creatinine value and is less informative for impending AKI than low eGFR. Early postoperative artifacts make high eGFR particularly unreliable as a protective signal.

Peak eGFR in this window is generally less predictive than nadir/mean because AKI risk is driven by worsening function. It may reflect transient improvement or dilution rather than true renal recovery.

Even close to the outcome, the maximum eGFR emphasizes best-case values and is typically less aligned with AKI pathophysiology than low/mean eGFR. Likely redundant and weaker than the 25–36h eGFR_mean signal.

Minimum eGFR in the immediate postop window reflects early renal function drop (often driven by creatinine changes). Strong renal specificity but early transient hemodilution/CPB effects warrant slight conservatism.

Minimum eGFR during the trajectory phase captures evolving renal dysfunction beyond immediate CPB/anesthesia effects. Good renal specificity and better prognostic proximity to later creatinine rise than 0–12h.

Minimum eGFR closest to the outcome window is a near-direct marker of evolving AKI and reduced renal reserve. High temporal proximity and extreme-value aggregation make it highly predictive of Hour-60 creatinine ratio.
Count/frequency of eGFR measurements mainly reflects monitoring intensity or clinician concern rather than physiology. Early postop labs are common, so this is a weak proxy signal.
Measurement frequency may indicate concern for renal dysfunction, but remains largely a care-process proxy. Moderate proxy value with limited mechanistic linkage.
More frequent eGFR checks late (25–36h) can signal suspected or recognized AKI close to the outcome. Still a monitoring-intensity proxy, but timing makes it more informative than earlier epochs.

LDH measurement frequency is primarily a clinician-behavior/diagnostic workup marker (hemolysis, ischemia) with weak direct renal specificity. Early postop ordering is common and nonspecific for predicting creatinine ratio.
LDH measurement count remains a nonspecific monitoring proxy and is not a direct AKI pathway marker. Limited incremental value for renal outcome prediction.

Late LDH monitoring may reflect concern for hemolysis/ischemia or ongoing instability that can contribute to AKI, but it is still indirect. Timing improves signal slightly, yet proxy penalty remains high.

Mean INR early postop reflects coagulation status (warfarin effect, liver dysfunction, dilution/CPB) and overall illness severity more than renal injury. Indirect association with AKI via bleeding/transfusion/shock pathways.

Persistently elevated mean INR later may indicate ongoing hepatic dysfunction, inflammation, or bleeding risk contributing to hemodynamic stress and AKI. Closer timing improves prognostic proximity, though renal specificity is moderate.

Peak INR in the immediate postop period often reflects transient CPB-related coagulopathy/dilution and is not kidney-specific. Extremes can mark severe derangement but are early and nonspecific for Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

Peak INR in the 25–36h window can capture significant ongoing coagulopathy/liver dysfunction or anticoagulation issues linked to bleeding/transfusion and shock, which can worsen renal perfusion. Still indirect but temporally closer and extreme aggregation is informative.
Minimum INR early is usually clinically uninformative for AKI risk and may reflect baseline/measurement variability. Low renal specificity and weak causal proximity.
Minimum INR later similarly provides little mechanistic information about renal injury compared with elevated INR metrics. Likely redundant and weakly related to creatinine trajectory.
