INR measurement frequency is largely a care-process marker (bleeding risk, anticoagulation management) rather than a renal mechanism. Early postop INR checks are routine, so predictive value for creatinine ratio is minimal.

INR measurement frequency mainly reflects bleeding/anticoagulation monitoring intensity after surgery rather than renal physiology. It may weakly correlate with overall instability/transfusion needs that can affect AKI risk, but is an indirect proxy.

INR measurement frequency in the 25–36h window is still primarily a care-process/monitoring signal (coagulopathy/anticoagulation concern), not a kidney-specific marker. Temporal proximity is better, but proxy penalty keeps it low.

Mean eosinophil percentage early post-op is nonspecific and commonly suppressed by stress steroids/catecholamines, with little direct linkage to AKI mechanisms. The mean in 0–12h is unlikely to add meaningful signal for Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

Eosinophil percentage is a weak, nonspecific inflammatory/allergic marker and not a reliable perioperative AKI predictor. Mean aggregation further dilutes any rare signal (e.g., drug reaction), so expected utility is minimal.

A peak eosinophil percentage could rarely flag allergic/drug-related interstitial nephritis or transfusion reaction, but this is uncommon and not a typical early post-cardiac surgery AKI pathway. Early (0–12h) timing and low specificity limit predictive value.

A later peak eosinophil percentage may weakly indicate evolving drug reaction/inflammation, but remains an indirect and uncommon driver of creatinine rise in this setting. Max is the best aggregation for rare spikes, yet overall renal specificity is low.
A low eosinophil percentage mainly reflects stress response and is not kidney-specific. The minimum is not a meaningful renal insult marker and is unlikely to predict Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

Minimum eosinophil percentage in 13–24h is still dominated by nonspecific stress physiology and offers little mechanistic connection to AKI. Redundant with other eosinophil summaries and low renal relevance.
Whether eosinophils were measured is largely a routine CBC ordering artifact and reflects monitoring rather than physiology. Measurement frequency in 0–12h is especially nonspecific post-op.
Eosinophil measurement presence is a care-process proxy (CBC obtained) with minimal kidney specificity. It may correlate with general acuity but is too indirect for creatinine ratio prediction.

Even close to the outcome window, eosinophil measurement presence mainly indicates labs were drawn rather than renal injury. Proxy penalty dominates and it should not contribute much to sparse prediction.

LDL measurement in the immediate post-op period is unrelated to acute renal perfusion or evolving AKI and mostly reflects baseline lipid management/workup. As a measured-only feature, it is a monitoring/process proxy with negligible relevance.
LDL measurement presence is not mechanistically tied to postoperative creatinine rise and is typically incidental to acute ICU care. It is a weak proxy at best and should score minimal importance.
LDL measurement presence near 25–36h remains a non-renal, non-acute process variable (lipid panel ordering). It provides essentially no direct signal for Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

Mean MCV reflects baseline anemia/macrocytosis, transfusion effects, or chronic nutritional/liver disease, which can correlate weakly with frailty and illness severity. It is not kidney-specific and early post-op mean values are only indirect predictors.

MCV in 13–24h may capture transfusion/hemodilution trajectory and chronic comorbidity burden, which can indirectly relate to AKI risk. Still lacks renal specificity and is a modest, nonspecific predictor.

Mean corpuscular volume (MCV) reflects RBC size and anemia/nutrition/transfusion effects rather than kidney function. Even in the closest window, it is an indirect severity/transfusion proxy with weak mechanistic linkage to AKI.

Peak MCV in the immediate postop period is usually driven by dilution, transfusion, or lab variability. It has minimal renal specificity and early transient changes are common after bypass/resuscitation.
Max MCV in 13–24h mainly reflects transfusion/macrocytosis patterns and is not a recognized near-term AKI precursor. Prognostic proximity to creatinine rise is low and largely nonspecific.
Later peak MCV may capture ongoing transfusion/hemodilution or inflammation, which can correlate with illness severity. Still, it is a weak, indirect proxy with low renal specificity.
Low MCV early postop is typically baseline microcytosis or dilutional effects and does not reflect renal perfusion or injury. Temporal proximity is poor and signal is nonspecific.
Minimum MCV in this window is largely a chronic hematologic characteristic or dilutional artifact. It has little causal/prognostic proximity to evolving AKI.
Low MCV near 36h may correlate with anemia/bleeding/transfusion burden, which can track overall stress and hypotension risk. However, it remains an indirect marker with limited renal specificity.
MCV 'measured' is primarily a monitoring-frequency/process-of-care variable (CBC ordering). It reflects clinician behavior more than renal pathophysiology and should be heavily proxy-penalized.
Frequency of MCV measurement indicates lab intensity rather than kidney injury mechanisms. Any association with creatinine is indirect via acuity/bleeding workup.
More frequent CBCs late may signal ongoing instability (bleeding, infection) that can co-occur with AKI risk. Still, it is a monitoring proxy and not a direct renal indicator.

Mean platelet volume (MPV) reflects platelet activation/inflammation and can relate to systemic stress after cardiopulmonary bypass. Renal specificity is low and early postop values are often transiently perturbed.
MPV in 13–24h may track inflammatory/consumptive physiology that correlates with AKI risk, but it is indirect. Mean aggregation may dilute short-lived extremes and remains nonspecific.

Sustained elevated MPV closer to outcome can reflect ongoing inflammation/platelet activation and microvascular dysfunction, which may accompany AKI. Still not a kidney biomarker, but timing improves prognostic value.

Peak MPV early may capture acute inflammatory/activation response to surgery/CPB, but this is common and nonspecific. Limited renal specificity and early timing reduce usefulness for Hour-60 creatinine.
Max MPV in 13–24h could reflect evolving systemic inflammation or sepsis-like physiology that can predispose to AKI. However, it remains an indirect proxy without strong causal proximity.

A high MPV peak in 25–36h may better capture ongoing inflammatory/microthrombotic stress nearer to the creatinine outcome. It is still indirect but has improved temporal proximity and extreme aggregation may preserve signal.

Minimum MPV in the immediate postop window is a nonspecific hematologic/inflammatory marker and not kidney-specific. Early extremes are common after CPB/transfusion and have weak, indirect linkage to Hour-60 creatinine.

Minimum MPV during 13–24h remains a nonspecific marker with limited mechanistic connection to AKI. Any association is indirect (overall inflammation/illness) and likely redundant/noisy for sparse prediction.

Late-window low MPV could weakly reflect ongoing inflammation/consumption that correlates with organ dysfunction, but it is not a renal biomarker. Temporal proximity helps slightly, yet renal specificity remains low.
MPV 'measured' mainly reflects lab ordering/monitoring intensity rather than physiology. This is a care-process proxy with minimal direct relevance to creatinine rise.
Measurement frequency is predominantly clinician behavior and does not encode a renal mechanism. At best it tracks general acuity, so it is weak for predicting creatinine_ratio.
Even close to the outcome, 'measured' is still a monitoring-intensity proxy and should not be treated as a physiologic predictor. Renal specificity is minimal.

Mean lactate reflects global hypoperfusion/oxygen debt after surgery, which is an established AKI risk pathway. In 0–12h it can be transient from CPB/anesthesia, so predictive value is moderate rather than high.

Persistently elevated mean lactate in 13–24h suggests ongoing shock/low cardiac output and impaired renal perfusion, a strong precursor to AKI. The trajectory phase is more prognostic than immediate postop values.

Peak lactate captures the worst early hypoperfusion insult, which can contribute to renal ischemia. However, early peaks are common and may resolve quickly, limiting specificity for Hour-60 creatinine.

A high lactate maximum in 13–24h indicates significant ongoing or recurrent hypoperfusion, closely linked to subsequent renal dysfunction. Using the extreme is appropriate because brief severe shock episodes can drive AKI.

Minimum lactate mainly reflects best-case perfusion and is less informative for injury risk than peaks or sustained elevation. In the immediate postop period it is particularly prone to being normal despite earlier insults.
A low minimum lactate does not rule out intermittent hypoperfusion and is less prognostic than mean/max for AKI risk. It provides only weak indirect information about renal perfusion adequacy.

Lactate measurement frequency is a proxy for clinician concern about shock/instability rather than a direct renal mechanism. It may weakly signal higher acuity early after surgery but is not a strong predictor.
More frequent lactate checks in 13–24h can indicate ongoing hemodynamic concern, indirectly associated with AKI risk. Still, it is primarily a monitoring-intensity proxy and should remain low.

Late frequent lactate monitoring may reflect persistent instability closer to the outcome, giving some indirect prognostic signal. Nonetheless it remains a care-process variable and should not score above 2.

Mean alkaline phosphatase is a hepatobiliary/bone marker with little direct relationship to renal injury or perfusion. Early postop changes are nonspecific and unlikely to add meaningful signal for creatinine_ratio.
Alkaline phosphatase reflects hepatobiliary/bone turnover rather than renal function. Early postoperative peaks are nonspecific and have weak mechanistic linkage to AKI/creatinine rise.
Low alkaline phosphatase is not a recognized near-term driver or marker of postoperative kidney injury. The minimum in the immediate postop window is unlikely to add renal-specific prognostic signal.
Whether ALP was measured mainly reflects lab-ordering/monitoring patterns rather than kidney physiology. In the 0–12h window it is especially prone to being a care-process proxy.
ALP measurement frequency is a clinician-behavior proxy (e.g., liver workup) with minimal renal specificity. Even in the trajectory phase it is not a meaningful AKI pathway indicator.

Despite being closer to the Hour-60 outcome, ALP being measured remains a monitoring-intensity signal with little direct connection to renal perfusion or injury. Per rules, _measured variables should stay low unless clearly renal-directed.

RDW reflects chronic inflammation, anemia, and physiologic stress and can correlate with worse outcomes, but it is not kidney-specific. The mean in the immediate postop period is a weak, indirect predictor of later creatinine rise.

RDW may capture ongoing systemic stress/inflammation that modestly associates with AKI risk, but remains nonspecific. Mean aggregation can dilute any transient hematologic signal relevant to renal hypoperfusion.

Closer timing modestly improves usefulness as a marker of persistent illness severity, but RDW is still an indirect proxy rather than a renal biomarker. Mean RDW is unlikely to be a strong sparse predictor of creatinine_ratio.

A high RDW can indicate baseline frailty/anemia and is associated with complications, but it does not directly reflect kidney injury. Early postop maxima are often driven by transfusion/hemodilution effects and are nonspecific.

Peak RDW in this window may reflect ongoing transfusion/hemoconcentration and systemic stress, which only indirectly relates to AKI. It is a moderate-to-weak prognostic proxy rather than a mechanistic renal signal.

A persistently high/peaking RDW closer to the outcome can mark sustained physiologic stress, inflammation, or anemia that correlates with AKI risk. Max aggregation better preserves adverse extremes than mean, though it remains nonspecific.
Low RDW is generally not a risk marker for AKI and has little renal specificity. The minimum value early after surgery is unlikely to carry meaningful prognostic information for creatinine_ratio.
RDW minima are not clinically tied to renal hypoperfusion or evolving kidney injury. This aggregation is largely uninformative and potentially redundant with other RDW summaries.
Even near the outcome window, a low RDW does not indicate renal injury or reduced renal reserve. The minimum is not a useful extreme for AKI prediction compared with max.
Whether RDW was measured mainly reflects CBC ordering frequency and monitoring intensity, not kidney physiology. In the immediate postop period this is especially a care-process proxy.

RDW measurement frequency is a nonspecific marker of clinician monitoring rather than a renal mechanism. Per rules, _measured features should be scored low unless clearly indicating renal-directed concern.

RDW measurement frequency is primarily a monitoring/ordering-intensity signal rather than a renal mechanism. As a _measured variable it is a weak proxy for illness severity and should not strongly predict Hour 60 creatinine ratio.

Mean chloride early post-op reflects fluid/acid-base management (e.g., saline load) with only indirect links to AKI risk. In the 0–12h window the mean can wash out brief hyperchloremic exposure and is less temporally proximal to Hour 60 creatinine.

Mean chloride in 13–24h may reflect ongoing hyperchloremia from resuscitation and is a modest, indirect AKI risk marker. Mean aggregation is less sensitive than extremes for capturing potentially nephrotoxic chloride loads.

Mean chloride in 25–36h is closer to the outcome and may capture sustained hyperchloremia/acid-base disturbance associated with renal vasoconstriction and AKI risk. Still indirect and treatment-influenced, so not top-tier.

Peak chloride in the immediate post-op period can indicate high chloride resuscitation and hyperchloremic acidosis, which has an indirect association with AKI. Early transient peaks are common after bypass/resuscitation, limiting prognostic specificity.

Peak chloride during 13–24h better reflects ongoing exposure to hyperchloremia beyond the immediate operative period. Extremes are more informative than means for capturing potentially harmful chloride loads, but remain indirect and care-driven.

Peak chloride in 25–36h is temporally closer and may signal persistent hyperchloremia/acid-base derangement associated with worse renal perfusion and AKI risk. It is still not a direct kidney function marker and is influenced by fluid choice.

Low chloride early post-op is usually nonspecific (dilution, diuretics, lab variation) and not a well-established AKI pathway. The 0–12h timing and minimum aggregation provide limited renal-specific prognostic value.

Minimum chloride in 13–24h is generally a weak, nonspecific electrolyte signal with limited mechanistic linkage to AKI compared with hyperchloremia. It is more reflective of dilution/therapy effects than renal injury.

Low chloride in 25–36h could reflect ongoing diuresis or metabolic alkalosis from therapy, which may correlate with clinical course but is not renal-specific. Closer timing gives slight added prognostic value, but it remains indirect.

Chloride measurement frequency is mainly a proxy for lab monitoring intensity and clinician concern rather than physiology. Per rules, _measured variables should score low and are not renal-mechanistic.

More frequent chloride checks in 13–24h reflect care processes (acid-base/electrolyte monitoring) and overall instability rather than kidney injury itself. As a _measured feature it has high proxy penalty.

In 25–36h, frequent chloride measurement may indicate ongoing metabolic/renal concern and closer-to-outcome instability, but it remains primarily a monitoring-intensity proxy. Per rules it should not exceed a low-to-moderate score.

Whether HDL was measured early post-op is an ordering/monitoring artifact with no direct renal specificity. It is unlikely to add meaningful signal for Hour 60 creatinine ratio beyond general care patterns.

HDL measurement in 13–24h is a clinician-behavior proxy (lipid panel ordering) and not mechanistically tied to AKI evolution. It should have minimal predictive value for postoperative creatinine rise.

HDL measurement in 25–36h remains a low-information care-process indicator rather than a renal biomarker or perfusion signal. Even with closer timing, it is not expected to predict creatinine ratio meaningfully.

Mean neutrophil percentage reflects early postoperative inflammation/stress or infection risk, which is only indirectly related to AKI. In the immediate 0–12h window it is nonspecific and diluted by averaging, so predictive value for Hour-60 creatinine ratio is limited.

Neutrophil percentage in 13–24h may capture evolving inflammatory response, but remains a nonspecific illness marker rather than a renal mechanism. Mean aggregation further washes out transient spikes that might matter more.

Peak neutrophil percentage can indicate acute stress/inflammation, which can correlate with complications that secondarily affect kidneys. However it is not renal-specific and early postoperative peaks are common, so it is a weak predictor.
A higher peak neutrophil percentage in 13–24h could signal infection/SIRS that may contribute to AKI risk. Still indirect and nonspecific, so only modest usefulness for predicting creatinine ratio.

Minimum neutrophil percentage is rarely a meaningful renal-perfusion or kidney-injury signal in this context. It is more likely noise or reflects differential shifts without clear linkage to Hour-60 creatinine change.

Low-end neutrophil percentage in 13–24h is not a recognized near-term precursor of AKI after cardiac surgery. It is largely nonspecific and unlikely to add predictive signal beyond other acuity markers.
Whether/ how often neutrophils were measured mainly reflects lab-ordering intensity and routine postoperative monitoring. This is a care-process proxy with minimal direct renal relevance.
Measurement frequency is primarily a proxy for clinician concern or protocolized labs rather than kidney physiology. It should contribute little to predicting Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

In the 25–36h window, repeated CBCs may reflect concern for infection/instability that can precede AKI, but it remains a monitoring-intensity proxy. Per rules, measured variables should stay low despite closer timing.

Albumin reflects oncotic pressure, capillary leak/inflammation, and hemodilution; hypoalbuminemia is associated with AKI risk and worse outcomes. In 0–12h it is influenced by fluids/CPB, so it is a moderate (not top-tier) predictor.
Maximum albumin early post-op is less informative for AKI than low albumin, and may reflect hemoconcentration rather than renal risk. Early timing and limited mechanistic linkage reduce usefulness.

Minimum albumin in 0–12h captures the worst hypoalbuminemia/hemodilution and systemic inflammation, which are associated with AKI and renal congestion risk. Still somewhat confounded by immediate postoperative fluid shifts, keeping it moderate.
Albumin measurement frequency is largely a clinician-ordering/monitoring proxy and often routine after cardiac surgery. It does not directly encode kidney injury or perfusion.
How often albumin is checked in 13–24h mainly reflects practice patterns or concern about nutrition/volume status rather than renal pathophysiology. Predictive value for creatinine ratio is minimal.

More frequent albumin checks closer to the outcome may indicate ongoing instability, capillary leak, or aggressive fluid/colloid management that can relate to AKI risk. However it remains a monitoring-intensity proxy, so it should stay low.

Whether triglycerides were measured is typically unrelated to renal perfusion or AKI pathways and often reflects nutrition/propofol monitoring. This is a weak care-process signal for Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

Whether triglycerides were checked in 13–24h is mainly a monitoring/TPN/propofol-related practice signal rather than a renal mechanism. As a _measured variable it is a weak, nonspecific proxy for acuity with little direct linkage to AKI at Hour 60.

Triglycerides being measured at 25–36h may reflect ongoing propofol infusion, nutrition evaluation, or clinician concern, not kidney function itself. Closer timing to the outcome gives slight prognostic value, but it remains primarily a monitoring-intensity proxy.

Mean hemoglobin in the immediate post-op window reflects anemia/hemodilution/transfusion context, which can influence renal oxygen delivery but is indirect for AKI. Early post-bypass values are often transient and the mean can wash out brief critical lows.

Mean hemoglobin at 13–24h captures ongoing anemia after initial resuscitation and may relate to renal oxygen delivery and overall recovery trajectory. Still indirect and mean aggregation is less sensitive than nadir for renal hypoperfusion risk.

Mean hemoglobin at 25–36h reflects sustained anemia close to the Hour 60 creatinine outcome, which can contribute to renal hypoxia and correlate with ongoing bleeding/transfusion burden. Timing is favorable, but it remains an indirect predictor compared with hemodynamics/renal biomarkers.

Peak hemoglobin early post-op is usually driven by transfusion or hemoconcentration and is not a recognized direct pathway to AKI. In the 0–12h window it is particularly confounded by bypass/resuscitation dynamics.
Maximum hemoglobin in 13–24h mainly reflects transfusion targets or hemoconcentration rather than renal injury risk. It has low renal specificity and is less informative than low hemoglobin extremes.

High hemoglobin at 25–36h is generally not a driver of postoperative creatinine rise and often reflects clinician transfusion strategy or volume status. Limited renal specificity and weak causal proximity to AKI.

Nadir hemoglobin in 0–12h can indicate significant anemia/bleeding/hemodilution, which may worsen renal oxygen delivery during a vulnerable period. However, early nadirs are common and transient post-CPB, so predictive value is moderated.

Lowest hemoglobin in 13–24h better reflects persistent anemia after initial stabilization and can be a meaningful stressor for renal oxygenation. Still an indirect AKI predictor and partly confounded by transfusion practices.

Nadir hemoglobin at 25–36h captures clinically important sustained/late anemia close to the outcome window, which can contribute to renal hypoxia and correlate with ongoing bleeding or inadequate resuscitation. The extreme (min) is the most pathophysiologically relevant aggregation for renal risk among hemoglobin summaries.

Frequency/occurrence of hemoglobin measurement early post-op reflects monitoring intensity, bleeding concern, or instability rather than kidney physiology. As a _measured feature it is a proxy signal and should not score highly.

Hemoglobin measurement frequency in 13–24h may indicate ongoing bleeding/transfusion evaluation and overall instability, indirectly associated with AKI risk. Still primarily a care-process proxy with limited renal specificity.

Continued hemoglobin checks at 25–36h can signal persistent bleeding, transfusion needs, or delayed instability closer to the creatinine outcome. Despite favorable timing, it remains a monitoring-intensity proxy and is capped low.

Whether TEG fibrinogen activity was measured early post-op reflects coagulation management/bleeding evaluation rather than renal function. It is a clinician-behavior proxy with minimal direct linkage to Hour 60 creatinine ratio.

TEG fibrinogen activity measurement in 13–24h indicates ongoing coagulation/bleeding assessment and practice patterns, not a renal injury pathway. As a _measured variable it has low specificity for predicting creatinine rise.

Indicates whether TEG fibrinogen activity was checked, largely reflecting monitoring/bleeding concern rather than renal physiology. As a _measured variable it is mainly clinician-behavior proxy and not a direct AKI pathway signal.

ACT reflects anticoagulation/heparin effect around bypass and early postop bleeding/thrombosis management. Mean ACT in the immediate 0–12h window is indirect for kidney injury and is confounded by protocolized care.

Peak ACT captures periods of intense anticoagulation (e.g., CPB/heparinization) and may correlate with surgical complexity. It is not kidney-specific and early extremes are often expected/managed, limiting prognostic value for Hour-60 creatinine.
Minimum ACT mainly reflects reversal/normalization of anticoagulation and lab timing rather than renal perfusion or injury. It has weak mechanistic linkage to subsequent creatinine rise.

Frequency/presence of ACT measurement is primarily a monitoring-intensity and procedural-context marker (e.g., ongoing anticoagulation management). Per rules, _measured variables are weak proxies and not direct renal predictors.

ACT being measured in 13–24h suggests continued anticoagulation/bleeding evaluation, reflecting clinician concern more than kidney physiology. As a _measured feature it is a low-specificity proxy for AKI risk.

ACT measurement in 25–36h may indicate ongoing anticoagulation management, but still represents care-process rather than renal mechanism. Despite closer timing, _measured variables should remain low importance.

AST reflects hepatic/ischemic injury and overall perioperative hypoperfusion burden, which can correlate with AKI risk. However it is nonspecific and early mean values may dilute short ischemic insults.

Peak AST in 0–12h can capture ischemic hepatitis/low-flow states and major operative insult, which are linked to renal hypoperfusion and later AKI. Still indirect and early-postop elevations can be transient, so not top-tier.
Minimum AST is generally not clinically meaningful for organ injury and is driven by baseline/lab noise. It has little renal specificity or prognostic proximity to creatinine rise.
AST measurement frequency mainly reflects monitoring intensity and clinician concern for liver injury/overall instability. As a _measured variable it is a weak, nonspecific proxy for AKI risk.

Checking AST in 13–24h indicates ongoing lab surveillance rather than a renal mechanism. Measurement presence is a low-specificity proxy and should not be weighted highly for predicting Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

AST being measured in the 25–36h window reflects continued monitoring and illness severity rather than kidney-specific injury. Per scoring rules, _measured features remain low even when temporally closer.

Total protein reflects hemodilution, nutritional/inflammatory status, and capillary leak, which can relate indirectly to fluid balance and renal congestion. Early mean values are nonspecific and heavily influenced by perioperative fluids.

Maximum total protein in the immediate postop period is usually not a meaningful pathologic extreme for AKI prediction and is affected by lab variability and transfusion/fluid shifts. It has low renal specificity.

Low total protein can indicate hemodilution/capillary leak and may correlate with aggressive resuscitation and edema, indirectly affecting renal function. However it is nonspecific and early postoperative lows are common, limiting predictive strength.

Measurement frequency of total protein is mainly a monitoring-intensity/proxy variable rather than a renal mechanism. Early postop timing and weak renal specificity make it minimally useful for predicting Hour-60 creatinine ratio.
Total protein measurement count reflects clinician behavior/acuity rather than kidney function or perfusion. Even in the trajectory phase it has low renal specificity and high proxy penalty.

Late-window measurement frequency may weakly signal evolving complications prompting broader lab surveillance, but it is still not a kidney biomarker. Proxy penalty remains high, so predictive value for creatinine_ratio is limited.

Whether HbA1c was checked reflects preexisting diabetes assessment/ordering patterns, not acute renal physiology. As a measured/ordering proxy in the immediate postop period, it adds little to Hour-60 creatinine prediction.
HbA1c measurement is largely unrelated to evolving AKI pathways and mostly indicates clinician workup patterns. It has low temporal and mechanistic proximity to creatinine rise at 60 hours.

Late HbA1c ordering may weakly correlate with comorbidity burden/diabetes concern, which affects renal reserve, but it is still an indirect proxy. As a _measured feature it should remain low importance.

Mean serum CO2 (bicarbonate) reflects acid-base status and can be affected by shock, ventilation, and early resuscitation; it is only indirectly linked to AKI. Early postoperative means can be transient and diluted, limiting prognostic value.

Sustained lower bicarbonate may indicate ongoing metabolic acidosis from hypoperfusion/sepsis that can contribute to AKI, but CO2 is nonspecific. Mean aggregation still washes out short insults and remains an indirect predictor.

In the 25–36h leading window, persistent metabolic acidosis (low CO2) is a stronger signal of ongoing hypoperfusion/critical illness that can precede creatinine rise. Still indirect and nonspecific, so not top-tier.

High CO2 (bicarbonate) early after surgery often reflects transient alkalosis from ventilation/diuretics or bicarbonate administration rather than renal injury. The extreme is not a well-established AKI precursor here.
Peak bicarbonate is generally a weak and nonspecific marker for subsequent AKI compared with low bicarbonate/acidosis. It is more influenced by treatment and ventilation than renal pathophysiology.

Late-window high bicarbonate may reflect diuretic/contraction alkalosis or bicarbonate therapy, which can correlate with illness/treatment patterns but not directly with AKI. Some weak association via treatment context, but largely indirect.

Minimum CO2 captures episodes of metabolic acidosis that can reflect hypoperfusion and impending organ dysfunction, a plausible AKI pathway. However, in the immediate postop period acidosis can be transient from bypass/resuscitation, so score is moderated.

A low CO2 nadir during 13–24h suggests persistent or worsening metabolic acidosis, which is more prognostically meaningful for AKI than early transient changes. Still indirect (not a kidney biomarker), so moderate importance.

Low CO2 nadir in the 25–36h window is a strong near-term indicator of ongoing metabolic acidosis from hypoperfusion/sepsis, closely preceding creatinine rise. Extreme aggregation captures clinically meaningful insults relevant to AKI risk.

Frequency of CO2 measurement reflects monitoring intensity and instability rather than renal physiology. In early postop care it may weakly proxy severity/shock risk, but proxy penalty keeps importance low.

Frequency of CO2 (total CO2/bicarbonate) checks mainly reflects monitoring intensity or clinician concern rather than a renal mechanism. As a _measured variable it is a weak, indirect proxy for instability and not specific for AKI.

More frequent CO2/bicarbonate measurement in the 25–36h window can signal evolving metabolic acidosis/instability closer to the creatinine outcome, but it remains a monitoring-intensity proxy. Per rules, _measured features are capped low and are not kidney-specific.

Whether BNP was checked early post-op mostly reflects clinician suspicion of heart failure/volume status rather than direct renal injury. In the immediate 0–12h period it is especially confounded by routine post-op practice and is not kidney-specific.

BNP measurement in 13–24h may indicate concern for persistent cardiac dysfunction/venous congestion, which can contribute to AKI, but the variable is still just a test-order proxy. As a _measured feature it provides weak, indirect prognostic information.

BNP being measured closer to the outcome can reflect ongoing concern for congestion/low output (cardiorenal physiology), which is somewhat relevant to later creatinine rise. However it remains a clinician-behavior proxy and cannot score high by the _measured rule.

Mean PaCO2 in the immediate post-op window largely reflects ventilation/anesthesia effects and is not specific to renal perfusion or injury. The mean also washes out short episodes of severe hypercapnia that would be more clinically meaningful.

Sustained hypercapnia in 13–24h can correlate with respiratory failure and hemodynamic stress that may indirectly affect renal function, but it is nonspecific. Using the mean reduces sensitivity to brief but important extremes.

In the 25–36h leading window, persistent hypercapnia may track ongoing critical illness and potential renal hypoperfusion risk, but it remains an indirect marker. Mean PaCO2 is still less informative than extremes for capturing acute insults.

Peak PaCO2 early post-op can capture episodes of hypoventilation/respiratory acidosis that may worsen pulmonary pressures and cardiac output, indirectly affecting kidneys. However 0–12h abnormalities are often transient and not renal-specific.

A high PaCO2 peak in 13–24h suggests significant respiratory failure or ventilatory issues, which can be associated with worse overall physiology and AKI risk. It is still an indirect, nonspecific pathway rather than a direct renal marker.

Peak PaCO2 in 25–36h is closer to the Hour-60 creatinine outcome and captures severe hypercapnic episodes that may reflect ongoing shock/low output or high ventilatory support needs. While not kidney-specific, the extreme is a better aggregation for clinically meaningful insult than the mean.
Low PaCO2 early post-op often reflects ventilator settings/hyperventilation and is not a recognized direct AKI pathway. In 0–12h it is highly confounded by routine perioperative management.

Minimum PaCO2 mainly indicates hyperventilation and is generally less tied to renal injury than hypercapnia or hemodynamic variables. It is a weak, nonspecific marker for the creatinine ratio outcome.

Even near the outcome window, low PaCO2 is usually a ventilation/acid-base management signal rather than renal perfusion or injury. The minimum is not a strong or specific precursor of AKI compared with other physiologic extremes.

Number/frequency of PaCO2 measurements reflects ABG monitoring intensity and ventilator management rather than kidney physiology. As a _measured feature in the immediate post-op period it is especially process-driven and weakly predictive.

More frequent PaCO2 checks in 13–24h can indicate ongoing respiratory instability and higher acuity, which may correlate with AKI risk indirectly. Still, it is primarily a clinician-behavior proxy and capped low by the _measured rule.

Frequency of PaCO2 measurement mainly reflects monitoring intensity/ventilator management rather than renal physiology. As a _measured proxy variable it has weak, indirect linkage to Hour-60 creatinine.

Whether indirect bilirubin was checked early post-op is largely a clinician-behavior/monitoring signal (concern for hemolysis/hepatic dysfunction) not kidney-specific. Early (0–12h) timing further limits specificity for later creatinine rise.
Measurement indicator for indirect bilirubin reflects testing intensity and nonspecific concern rather than a renal injury pathway. Even in 13–24h, it is an indirect proxy with low renal specificity.

Indirect bilirubin measurement in the 25–36h window may correlate with ongoing hemolysis/hepatic congestion and overall instability, which can co-occur with AKI. Still primarily a monitoring proxy, so it remains low importance.

Mean calcium in the immediate post-op period reflects transfusion/citrate load, albumin shifts, and resuscitation rather than kidney function directly. The mean can wash out brief extremes and early derangements are often transient.

Average calcium during 13–24h may weakly track ongoing critical illness, transfusion burden, or endocrine/metabolic status, but is not kidney-specific. Mean aggregation is less sensitive to clinically meaningful extremes relevant to AKI.

Mean calcium closer to the outcome may reflect persistent metabolic derangement, but remains an indirect and nonspecific predictor of creatinine change. It is more a general severity/metabolic proxy than a renal mechanism.

Peak calcium early after surgery is usually driven by supplementation and lab variability, with little direct linkage to renal perfusion or injury. Early post-op extremes are often iatrogenic/transient and weakly predictive of Hour-60 creatinine.
Maximum calcium in 13–24h is typically influenced by replacement practices and does not map to a clear AKI pathway. It is largely a treatment/measurement artifact rather than a renal biomarker.
Peak calcium in 25–36h still mainly reflects supplementation and does not directly indicate evolving kidney injury. Despite temporal proximity, renal specificity and causal proximity are low.

Low calcium early post-op can reflect citrate from transfusion, hemodilution, or critical illness and may correlate weakly with severity and hemodynamic stress. However it is not kidney-specific and early abnormalities are often transient.

Minimum calcium in 13–24h may better capture persistent hypocalcemia from ongoing transfusion/critical illness, which can accompany shock states linked to AKI. Still indirect with modest renal specificity.

Minimum calcium in 25–36h can signal ongoing critical illness (e.g., persistent transfusion/citrate load, inflammation) closer to the creatinine outcome and may track sustained instability associated with AKI. It remains indirect, but the extreme and timing make it a moderate predictor.

Calcium measurement frequency is primarily a monitoring/treatment-intensity proxy (electrolyte replacement, transfusion management) rather than renal physiology. As a _measured feature it should be low importance.
How often calcium is checked reflects clinician behavior and ICU protocol intensity more than kidney injury risk. It provides little direct information about renal function trajectory.

More frequent calcium checks in 25–36h may indicate ongoing instability, transfusion, or electrolyte replacement needs that can co-occur with AKI risk. Still a monitoring proxy, so importance remains low.

Mean PTT in the immediate postop window reflects anticoagulation/coagulopathy and bleeding risk more than renal physiology. It is an indirect severity/procedure proxy and early means can wash out short extremes relevant to renal hypoperfusion.

Later mean PTT may capture ongoing anticoagulation or hepatic dysfunction/consumption, which can correlate with illness severity but is not kidney-specific. Temporal proximity helps slightly, but it remains an indirect proxy for AKI risk.

Peak PTT early after surgery often reflects heparin effect, transfusion, or coagulopathy rather than a renal injury pathway. Extremes are better than means, but in 0–12h this is commonly transient and nonspecific for creatinine rise.

A high PTT at 25–36h suggests persistent anticoagulation/coagulopathy and may track with complicated ICU course. It is still not mechanistically close to kidney function, so predictive value for creatinine_ratio is likely modest.

Low PTT is usually not a meaningful signal for impending AKI and may reflect lab variability or reduced anticoagulation. It has weak renal specificity and high nonspecificity in the immediate postop period.

A low PTT later is generally not a direct marker of renal perfusion or kidney injury. It is more reflective of anticoagulation status and offers little specific information for Hour-60 creatinine change.
PTT measurement frequency mainly reflects monitoring intensity and anticoagulation management rather than physiology. This is a care-process proxy with minimal direct linkage to creatinine_ratio.
How often PTT is checked in 13–24h primarily indicates clinician concern/anticoagulation protocols. It is not kidney-specific and is prone to proxy penalty.

More frequent PTT checks at 25–36h may signal ongoing instability (bleeding, ECMO/assist devices, heparin titration) that can co-occur with AKI risk. Still, it is a monitoring-intensity proxy, so it should not score high.
Mean monocyte count early postop reflects inflammatory response/stress but is not a direct renal biomarker. It is nonspecific and early postoperative leukocyte shifts are common and transient.

Monocyte count in 13–24h may reflect evolving inflammation/infection, which can contribute indirectly to AKI risk. However, it remains a weak, nonspecific predictor compared with hemodynamics or renal labs.
Peak monocyte count in 0–12h is largely a nonspecific postoperative inflammatory signal. It has low renal specificity and limited prognostic proximity to creatinine_ratio.

A higher monocyte peak in 13–24h could indicate sustained inflammation or early infection, indirectly increasing AKI risk. Still, it is far from a kidney-specific pathway and likely adds limited incremental value.
Low monocyte counts early are usually not a meaningful AKI signal and may reflect dilutional effects or lab noise. Renal specificity and causal proximity are minimal.
A low monocyte nadir in 13–24h is not a recognized near-term marker of renal hypoperfusion or tubular injury. It is nonspecific and unlikely to predict creatinine_ratio well.
Measurement frequency of monocyte count is essentially CBC monitoring intensity rather than a physiologic mechanism. It carries strong proxy penalty and little direct renal relevance.

Count of whether/ how often monocytes were checked in the 13–24h window, mainly reflecting monitoring intensity. This is a weak, nonspecific proxy with high clinician-behavior signal and little direct renal mechanistic linkage.

Measurement frequency of monocyte counts in the 25–36h window, which mostly captures lab-ordering behavior rather than physiology. Even though temporally closer to Hour 60, it remains a low-specificity proxy for AKI risk.

Average serum phosphorus early after surgery can reflect renal clearance and cell shifts, but is often confounded by transfusions, nutrition, and perioperative physiology. Mean in the immediate 0–12h period may wash out brief abnormalities and is not kidney-specific enough for a high score.

Mean phosphorus in 13–24h may begin to reflect evolving renal dysfunction, but remains indirect and influenced by non-renal factors. Mean aggregation reduces sensitivity to clinically important peaks that better track impaired excretion.

Mean phosphorus in the 25–36h leading-indicator window can signal reduced renal excretion as AKI evolves. Still indirect compared with creatinine/urine output, but timing improves prognostic proximity.

Peak phosphorus in the immediate postoperative window can indicate impaired renal clearance or tissue breakdown, but early peaks are commonly transient and confounded by perioperative shifts. Max is the right aggregation for renal signal, but timing limits specificity.

Peak phosphorus during 13–24h is a more meaningful marker of impaired excretion/ongoing injury than early postoperative values. Max captures the pathologic extreme better than mean and has moderate renal relevance.

Peak phosphorus in 25–36h is a strong near-term indicator of reduced renal clearance and evolving AKI physiology. The max aggregation preserves clinically important extremes and is temporally close to the Hour 60 creatinine ratio.

Lowest phosphorus early after surgery more often reflects dilution, insulin/glucose shifts, or refeeding-related effects rather than kidney injury. It has low renal specificity and limited prognostic value for later creatinine rise.
Minimum phosphorus in 13–24h is typically driven by non-renal shifts and treatment effects, not impaired renal excretion. As a low extreme, it is weakly connected to AKI mechanisms.

Minimum phosphorus in 25–36h remains a nonspecific signal (often dilutional or intracellular shift) and does not track the typical direction of renal failure physiology. Despite proximity, it is unlikely to add meaningful predictive power for creatinine_ratio.

Whether/how often phosphorus was measured in 0–12h mainly reflects routine post-op lab protocols and clinician behavior. Measurement frequency is not a physiologic renal marker and should be heavily proxy-penalized.

Phosphorus measurement frequency in 13–24h is largely a care-process variable and correlates more with monitoring intensity than kidney function. It provides minimal direct information about impending creatinine rise.

More frequent phosphorus checks in 25–36h may weakly indicate clinician concern for renal dysfunction or metabolic derangements. Still primarily a monitoring-intensity proxy, so it should not score above 2.

Mean neutrophil count early post-op reflects inflammation/stress response and possible infection risk, which can contribute indirectly to AKI. However it is nonspecific and early postoperative leukocytosis is common, limiting renal prognostic specificity.

Mean neutrophil count in 13–24h may better reflect sustained inflammation/infection than 0–12h and thus indirectly relate to AKI risk. Still a distal, nonspecific marker with substantial confounding and limited direct renal linkage.

Peak neutrophil count reflects early postoperative inflammation/stress or infection risk, which can contribute indirectly to AKI via systemic inflammation. In the immediate 0–12h window it is nonspecific and often transient, so renal prognostic proximity is limited.

Persistently high neutrophils in 13–24h may better indicate evolving inflammatory response or infection than the immediate postop period. Still an indirect, nonspecific AKI correlate rather than a renal/perfusion marker.

Minimum neutrophil count early after surgery is usually driven by dilution, lab timing, or perioperative effects rather than kidney-specific physiology. The min aggregation is not a meaningful AKI pathway signal here.

A low neutrophil nadir in 13–24h is not a recognized direct predictor of creatinine rise and is more reflective of lab variability or hematologic factors. Weak renal specificity and poor causal proximity.
Measurement frequency mainly reflects monitoring intensity and routine postop lab protocols rather than renal mechanism. Strong proxy penalty and no direct linkage to Hour-60 creatinine ratio.
How often ANC is checked is largely clinician/workflow driven and not kidney-specific. At most it signals general concern, so predictive value for creatinine_ratio is minimal.

More frequent ANC checks at 25–36h may indicate concern for infection/sepsis, which can precipitate AKI, making it a slightly more informative proxy near the outcome. Still a monitoring-intensity variable, so capped low.
Troponin measurement frequency reflects postoperative cardiac monitoring protocols and clinician concern rather than renal physiology. As a _measured feature it is primarily a process-of-care proxy.

Repeated troponin checks in 13–24h may track suspected myocardial injury, but the frequency itself is not a direct renal risk marker. High proxy penalty and limited incremental value for creatinine_ratio.

More frequent troponin monitoring at 25–36h can proxy ongoing hemodynamic/cardiac instability that may reduce renal perfusion. Still not a physiologic troponin value and remains a clinician-behavior signal, so low importance.

Mean PaO2 reflects oxygenation status; severe hypoxemia can contribute to systemic stress and renal hypoxia indirectly. In 0–12h it is heavily influenced by ventilation/FiO2 management and mean may wash out brief hypoxemic insults.

Sustained oxygenation in 13–24h may better reflect recovery vs ongoing respiratory failure, which can correlate with AKI risk via illness severity. However PaO2 is not kidney-specific and mean aggregation is only moderately informative.

Mean PaO2 at 25–36h is closer to the Hour-60 creatinine outcome and may capture ongoing respiratory failure/poor oxygen delivery that can worsen renal function. Still indirect and confounded by ventilator/FiO2 titration, so not top-tier.
Maximum PaO2 usually reflects high FiO2/ventilator settings rather than a pathologic exposure relevant to AKI. Poor renal specificity and the extreme is more about treatment than injury.
Peak PaO2 in 13–24h is largely driven by oxygen administration and does not capture hypoxemic burden. Minimal mechanistic linkage to creatinine rise and high treatment-proxy component.

Maximum PaO2 near 25–36h remains primarily a marker of supplemental oxygen strategy rather than renal perfusion or injury. The max aggregation is not aligned with the clinically relevant insult (hypoxemia), so low utility.

Minimum PaO2 in the immediate postop window reflects hypoxemia/respiratory failure and overall physiologic stress. It is only indirectly linked to AKI (via hypoxia/shock) and early transient derangements are common after surgery.
Minimum PaO2 during 13–24h captures persistent or recurrent hypoxemia beyond the immediate postop period. Still an indirect renal predictor, but more informative than 0–12h if hypoxemia persists.

Minimum PaO2 at 25–36h is closer to the Hour-60 creatinine outcome and may reflect ongoing respiratory failure with downstream renal hypoperfusion/inflammation. Extremes are clinically meaningful, but renal specificity remains moderate.

Frequency of PaO2 measurement mainly reflects monitoring intensity (ventilation changes, instability) rather than a renal mechanism. Strong proxy penalty and limited direct linkage to creatinine rise.
PaO2 measurement count in 13–24h is largely clinician behavior/acuity monitoring rather than kidney-specific physiology. At best a weak proxy for severity, not a direct AKI pathway.
More frequent PaO2 checks at 25–36h may indicate ongoing instability closer to the outcome window. Still primarily a monitoring-intensity proxy, so capped at low importance.

Mean potassium early after surgery reflects electrolyte balance influenced by shifts, transfusion, acid-base status, and renal handling. It is only an indirect AKI signal and the mean can wash out brief clinically important hyperkalemia.

Mean potassium at 13–24h can reflect evolving renal dysfunction or ongoing tissue breakdown/acid-base issues after initial stabilization. Moderate renal relevance, but mean aggregation is less sensitive than extremes.

Mean potassium at 25–36h is temporally closer and may reflect reduced renal excretion or sustained catabolic/acidotic state. Still not a direct kidney function biomarker and may be confounded by supplementation/diuretics.

Peak potassium in 0–12h captures clinically meaningful hyperkalemia, which can occur with early renal hypoperfusion, hemolysis, or acidosis. Early timing limits specificity because transient postoperative shifts are common.

Peak potassium in 13–24h is a stronger signal of impaired renal excretion or ongoing injury once immediate postoperative shifts should be settling. Max aggregation appropriately captures the pathologic extreme relevant to AKI risk.

Peak potassium at 25–36h is a near-term indicator of reduced renal clearance and/or worsening systemic illness close to the Hour-60 creatinine. Extremes are informative, though still influenced by treatment (supplementation, dialysis, diuretics).
Minimum potassium early reflects hypokalemia from diuresis, insulin, alkalosis, or dilution rather than kidney injury per se. It has weak and nonspecific association with later creatinine rise.

Minimum potassium in 13–24h is more likely driven by replacement strategies and diuretic use than intrinsic renal dysfunction. Limited renal specificity and substantial clinician-behavior confounding.

Minimum potassium at 25–36h remains a weak predictor of creatinine ratio because hypokalemia is usually treatment-related and not a typical AKI biomarker. Temporal proximity helps slightly but renal specificity is low.

Potassium measurement frequency reflects monitoring intensity (arrhythmia risk, electrolyte management, instability) more than renal pathophysiology. It may weakly proxy concern for renal/electrolyte issues but is not mechanistic.

Frequency of potassium checks in 13–24h is mainly a monitoring-intensity/clinical concern proxy (arrhythmia risk, renal dysfunction, acid-base issues). It is only indirectly related to AKI and should not be weighted highly versus physiologic potassium values.

Potassium measurement frequency closer to the outcome may reflect evolving renal dysfunction or instability, but it remains a care-process proxy rather than a renal biomarker. Per rules, _measured variables are capped low despite better temporal proximity.

Mean bilirubin early post-op reflects hepatic congestion/ischemia or hemolysis from bypass and overall shock burden, which can correlate with AKI risk. In 0–12h it is often transient and nonspecific, and the mean can dilute short severe insults.

Peak bilirubin in the immediate post-op window can capture early hypoperfusion/hemolysis severity, which is indirectly associated with later AKI. However it is not kidney-specific and early elevations are common after cardiac surgery, limiting prognostic proximity.

Minimum bilirubin in 0–12h is unlikely to encode renal injury pathways and mostly reflects baseline/normal values. It has poor causal proximity to creatinine rise and adds little beyond other severity markers.

Bilirubin measurement frequency immediately post-op is primarily a clinician-behavior/monitoring proxy (concern for liver injury/hemolysis). It is not a physiologic renal signal and should score very low.

How often bilirubin is checked in 13–24h reflects ongoing concern for hepatic dysfunction rather than kidney-specific injury. As a _measured feature it is largely a proxy and not a direct predictor of Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

More frequent bilirubin checks in 25–36h may indicate persistent multiorgan dysfunction closer to the creatinine outcome. Still, it is a monitoring-intensity proxy and cannot be scored high per the _measured rule.

Mean RBC count early post-op reflects anemia/hemodilution and bleeding/transfusion context, which can influence renal oxygen delivery indirectly. In 0–12h it is heavily affected by bypass prime and resuscitation, reducing specificity for later AKI.
Average RBC count in 13–24h captures ongoing anemia after stabilization, which may modestly relate to renal hypoxia risk. It remains indirect and the mean may miss clinically important nadirs.

Sustained anemia by 25–36h is closer to the outcome and may reflect ongoing bleeding/hemodilution or inadequate oxygen delivery that can worsen renal perfusion. Still indirect to kidney injury, but temporal proximity makes it a moderate predictor.

Maximum RBC count in 0–12h is usually not pathologic and may reflect hemoconcentration or transfusion, offering little mechanistic link to AKI. It is less informative than anemia/nadir measures for renal risk.
Higher RBC maxima in 13–24h generally do not indicate renal injury pathways and are often driven by transfusion or concentration effects. Prognostic proximity to creatinine rise is weak.

A high RBC maximum in 25–36h is not a typical driver of AKI and is less clinically meaningful than low RBC/anemia for renal oxygen delivery. It is likely redundant/noisy relative to mean or minimum RBC measures.

RBC nadir in 0–12h captures acute anemia from bleeding/hemodilution and transfusion needs, which can contribute to renal hypoxia and AKI risk. Despite early-window noise, the extreme (min) is the most pathophysiologically relevant aggregation in this family.

Minimum RBC count in 13–24h reflects persistent or worsening anemia after initial resuscitation, a more informative trajectory-phase signal for renal oxygen delivery risk. The nadir is a better predictor than mean/max and has moderate proximity to Hour-60 creatinine.

Low RBC count reflects anemia/bleeding/hemodilution and overall postoperative stress, which can correlate with AKI risk via oxygen delivery but is not kidney-specific. The 25–36h minimum captures a potentially meaningful nadir, yet remains an indirect proxy.

Whether RBC count was measured mainly reflects monitoring intensity and perioperative workflow rather than renal physiology. Early (0–12h) measurement is common after cardiac surgery, further reducing specificity for predicting Hour-60 creatinine ratio.
RBC measurement frequency is largely a care-process/acuity proxy and not a direct AKI pathway marker. In 13–24h it may reflect ongoing instability or bleeding surveillance, but renal linkage is weak.

RBC measurement in the 25–36h window can signal persistent instability, bleeding, or transfusion evaluation closer to the outcome window. Still, it is primarily a clinician-behavior proxy rather than a renal mechanism.

D-dimer being measured is a monitoring/diagnostic-intensity signal (e.g., concern for thrombosis/bleeding) rather than kidney function. In the immediate postoperative period it is commonly ordered and nonspecific for AKI prediction.

This indicates clinician concern for thrombotic/bleeding complications and overall acuity, not a direct renal biomarker. Any association with AKI would be indirect (inflammation, shock), so predictive value is limited.

Later D-dimer measurement may reflect evolving complications (thrombosis, DIC, severe inflammation) that can secondarily affect renal perfusion and injury. However it remains a measurement/proxy variable and is not kidney-specific.

TEG ACT being measured reflects coagulation monitoring and transfusion/bleeding management rather than renal physiology. In 0–12h post-cardiac surgery it is routine and poorly specific for subsequent creatinine rise.

This is primarily a care-process marker for ongoing coagulopathy/bleeding evaluation, with only indirect links to AKI through severity of illness. Measurement presence alone adds little renal-specific prognostic information.

TEG ACT measurement closer to Hour 60 may indicate persistent coagulopathy/bleeding requiring transfusion or hemodynamic support, which can contribute indirectly to AKI risk. Still, it is a monitoring-intensity proxy and not a direct renal signal.

Immature granulocytes reflect systemic inflammation/stress or infection, which can contribute to AKI via inflammatory pathways but is nonspecific. The early mean may be confounded by immediate postoperative inflammatory response and dilution.

A sustained elevation in immature granulocytes suggests ongoing inflammation/infection, a moderate indirect risk factor for AKI. Mean aggregation may dilute peaks, and renal specificity remains limited.

Peak immature granulocytes can capture an acute inflammatory surge, which may correlate with later AKI but is not kidney-specific. In 0–12h, postoperative inflammation is common, so prognostic specificity is modest.

A high peak in the 13–24h trajectory window may better indicate evolving infection/SIRS rather than routine post-bypass response, which can meaningfully increase AKI risk. Max aggregation preserves clinically relevant extremes compared with mean.

The minimum immature granulocyte percentage is not clinically informative for AKI mechanisms and mainly reflects absence of inflammation. As an early postoperative minimum, it has little prognostic value for creatinine ratio.
A low minimum immature granulocyte value provides minimal signal about renal injury risk and is largely uninformative compared with peaks. It does not capture the inflammatory burden relevant to AKI.

Measurement frequency of immature granulocyte % is mainly a monitoring-intensity/sepsis-workup proxy rather than a renal mechanism. Early postop timing and “_measured” aggregation add strong proxy penalty for predicting Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

Counts how often immature granulocyte % was checked, reflecting clinician concern/infection evaluation more than kidney injury itself. “_measured” variables are weak and indirect for AKI prediction even in the trajectory window.

Later measurement frequency may correlate with evolving instability/infection that can contribute to AKI, but it remains a care-process proxy. Closest window modestly increases usefulness, yet “_measured” keeps it low.

Whether TEG fibrinolysis was measured reflects bleeding/coagulopathy evaluation and clinician behavior, not kidney function. Immediate postop period further limits specificity for Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

TEG measurement is largely driven by transfusion/bleeding management rather than renal perfusion or tubular injury. As a measured/process indicator it has minimal direct prognostic proximity to creatinine rise.

Late TEG measurement may signal ongoing bleeding/transfusion needs that can associate with AKI risk, but it is still indirect and behavior-driven. Timing is closer to outcome, yet proxy penalty keeps importance low.

Mean early postoperative creatinine ratio is a direct kidney function marker and captures early AKI signal. However, immediate postop hemodilution/resuscitation can transiently distort creatinine, so it’s strong but not maximal.

Creatinine ratio in the 13–24h window reflects evolving renal function and recovery vs deterioration after surgery. Mean may smooth peaks, but it remains a direct biomarker with good prognostic proximity.
Creatinine ratio at 25–36h is a direct renal biomarker and the closest available snapshot to the Hour-60 outcome. Sustained elevation in this window strongly predicts subsequent creatinine at 60h.

Peak creatinine ratio in the first 12h can capture early renal insult, but early postoperative fluid shifts and lab timing can create transient extremes. Max is a good aggregation for acute injury, yet timing warrants some conservatism.

Peak creatinine ratio during 13–24h captures clinically meaningful worsening beyond immediate bypass/anesthesia effects. As an extreme of a direct renal biomarker, it is a strong near-term indicator of evolving AKI.

Peak creatinine ratio in the 25–36h leading-indicator window is highly proximal to the Hour-60 creatinine ratio and reflects ongoing kidney injury. Max aggregation preserves clinically important deterioration and is strongly predictive.

Minimum creatinine ratio early after surgery often reflects hemodilution or aggressive fluid resuscitation rather than improved renal function. As an extreme in the ‘better’ direction, it is less mechanistically tied to later creatinine rise.

A low minimum creatinine ratio in 13–24h may still be influenced by dilution and does not capture renal injury as well as max/mean. It is a direct biomarker but the chosen extreme is less informative for predicting worsening.

Minimum creatinine ratio in the closest window can indicate absence of sustained injury, but it is still less sensitive to AKI progression than max. Direct renal marker and temporal proximity support moderate importance.

Number/frequency of creatinine measurements reflects clinician concern and monitoring intensity rather than renal physiology. It may weakly correlate with instability/AKI suspicion, but as “_measured” in the early window it remains low value.

Indicates how often creatinine ratio was checked in the 13–24h window, reflecting monitoring intensity/clinician concern rather than renal physiology. As a proxy signal it may correlate with evolving AKI risk but should not be high-impact.

Measurement frequency of creatinine ratio in the 25–36h (closest) window is still primarily a care-process proxy. Temporal proximity may add a little predictive value, but it is not a direct kidney biomarker.

Use of 25% albumin early post-op often reflects volume management/third spacing or hypotension, indirectly related to renal perfusion. In 0–12h it is heavily confounded by routine resuscitation and clinician practice.

25% albumin use in 13–24h may signal ongoing capillary leak, low oncotic pressure, or hemodynamic instability that can affect renal perfusion. Still largely a treatment-choice proxy with modest renal specificity.

25% albumin use in 25–36h suggests persistent intravascular depletion/vasoplegia or fluid management challenges closer to the outcome window. Timing improves prognostic proximity, but it remains an indirect, clinician-mediated marker.

5% albumin dose early post-op reflects resuscitation/volume expansion and general hemodynamic management rather than a kidney-specific pathway. The immediate post-bypass period is noisy, limiting specificity for later creatinine rise.

Ongoing 5% albumin in 13–24h can indicate continued fluid needs and instability that may contribute to renal hypoperfusion. However it is a nonspecific therapy variable with substantial practice-pattern confounding.

5% albumin use in 25–36h may capture persistent hemodynamic/volume issues closer to Hour 60, which can precede AKI. Still indirect and partly redundant with other perfusion/volume markers, so not top-tier.
Analgesic dosing mainly reflects pain control/sedation strategy and is not a recognized driver of AKI in this context. Any association with creatinine change would be weak and highly confounded.
Continued acetaminophen-codeine use is a nonspecific care variable with minimal renal mechanistic linkage. It is unlikely to add meaningful signal for Hour 60 creatinine ratio.

Analgesic dosing in 25–36h remains largely unrelated to renal injury pathways and mostly reflects clinician preference and patient comfort. Predictive value for creatinine trajectory should be minimal.
Acetaminophen dosing is generally not nephrotoxic at therapeutic doses and does not directly reflect renal perfusion or injury. Any correlation would be indirect and weak.
Represents routine analgesia/antipyresis rather than kidney function or AKI risk. High proxy penalty and low renal specificity.
Ongoing acetaminophen use close to the outcome window still does not encode renal injury or perfusion failure. Expected contribution to sparse linear prediction of creatinine ratio is negligible.

Allopurinol use typically reflects pre-existing gout/hyperuricemia and may indirectly mark chronic kidney disease or reduced renal reserve. Perioperative dosing itself is not a near-term AKI signal, so predictive strength is limited.

Continuation of allopurinol in 13–24h is mainly a chronic-medication marker rather than an acute renal physiology indicator. It may weakly proxy baseline renal reserve/comorbidity but has low temporal proximity to evolving AKI mechanisms.

Allopurinol use/dose mainly reflects chronic gout/hyperuricemia management rather than an acute renal perfusion or injury signal. In the 25–36h window it is still largely a care-process/comorbidity proxy with weak mechanistic linkage to Hour-60 creatinine rise.

Aminocaproic acid is an antifibrinolytic given perioperatively for bleeding risk, indirectly marking surgical complexity/transfusion risk. Renal relevance is mostly indirect (hemodilution/bleeding/shock proxies) and early 0–12h dosing is common and nonspecific.

Continued aminocaproic acid in 13–24h suggests ongoing bleeding concern or re-exploration risk, which can correlate with hypotension/transfusion and AKI risk. Still primarily a proxy for severity/management rather than a kidney-specific pathway.

Use in 25–36h is less routine and may indicate persistent bleeding/coagulopathy with potential renal hypoperfusion exposure. However it remains an indirect treatment marker with substantial proxy penalty and no direct renal biomarker content.

Amiodarone dosing reflects postoperative atrial/ventricular arrhythmias and attempts at rhythm control, which can relate to hemodynamic instability. In 0–12h it is common and often prophylactic, making it a weak, indirect predictor of AKI-related creatinine rise.

Amiodarone in 13–24h more likely reflects clinically significant arrhythmia burden that can impair cardiac output and renal perfusion. Still indirect and confounded by clinician practice patterns, so only modest predictive value.

Ongoing amiodarone at 25–36h suggests persistent arrhythmias or low-output physiology closer to the Hour-60 creatinine outcome. This is a moderate near-term perfusion proxy (not kidney-specific), so it can contribute to prediction in a sparse model.

Amlodipine is a chronic antihypertensive and its early postoperative administration is uncommon and not tightly linked to AKI mechanisms. It mainly reflects home-med reconciliation/clinician choice rather than renal injury or perfusion failure.

Amlodipine use in 13–24h is largely a chronic BP-management signal and may even be held during instability, making it a noisy care-process proxy. It has minimal direct relevance to predicting creatinine ratio at Hour 60.
Amlodipine in 25–36h typically indicates clinical stability and transition back to home meds rather than evolving AKI. Any association with creatinine change is indirect and likely weak.

Ampicillin administration reflects antibiotic selection/prophylaxis or suspected infection rather than a direct renal function marker. In 0–12h it is especially driven by protocol and has low specificity for subsequent creatinine rise.

Ampicillin in 13–24h may indicate evolving infection concern, which can contribute to inflammation/shock and AKI risk. However it is still a treatment-choice proxy and ampicillin is not a classic nephrotoxin, so predictive value is limited.

Continued ampicillin at 25–36h can mark ongoing infection/sepsis evaluation closer to the outcome window, which can affect renal perfusion. The signal remains indirect and confounded by prescribing practices, keeping importance modest.

Angiotensin II use indicates refractory vasodilatory shock requiring high-level vasopressor support, a plausible pathway to renal hypoperfusion and AKI. In 0–12h it may reflect transient post-bypass vasoplegia, so it is informative but not top-tier.

Angiotensin II requirement in 13–24h suggests persistent vasoplegic shock and sustained renal perfusion risk beyond immediate post-op effects. This is a strong near-term physiologic severity marker with clear linkage to AKI risk.

Angiotensin II use/dose in 25–36h is a leading indicator of ongoing shock close to Hour 60, strongly associated with renal hypoperfusion and evolving AKI. While still a treatment proxy, its specificity for severe hemodynamic failure supports a high score.

Apixaban exposure mainly reflects anticoagulation strategy/bleeding-thrombosis risk rather than renal perfusion or injury. Early postoperative dosing is uncommon and is largely a care-process proxy with minimal direct linkage to Hour-60 creatinine rise.

Apixaban dosing in this window reflects clinician choice to resume anticoagulation and patient stability, not a kidney-specific pathway. Any association with creatinine is indirect (renal dosing/selection effects) and weak.

Later apixaban use may weakly proxy renal function because clinicians avoid/adjust DOACs with worsening kidney function. Still primarily a treatment-selection signal rather than a mechanistic predictor of subsequent creatinine increase.
Aspirin administration is routine post-cardiac surgery and mostly reflects protocolized antiplatelet care. It has little direct renal specificity and early dosing is not a strong AKI precursor.
Aspirin in this epoch remains largely protocol-driven and not kidney-specific. Any relationship to creatinine is indirect via overall recovery/bleeding risk rather than renal injury mechanisms.
Continued aspirin use at 25–36h is still a routine care marker with minimal renal mechanistic relevance. It is unlikely to add sparse predictive value for Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

Statin use reflects baseline cardiovascular disease management and may have modest anti-inflammatory/pleiotropic associations with AKI risk. However, it is not a direct renal biomarker and early postoperative dosing is a weak, indirect predictor.

Atorvastatin continuation in this window mainly indicates chronic therapy resumption and clinical stability. Renal linkage is indirect and modest, so predictive utility for creatinine trajectory is limited.

Later statin dosing may weakly correlate with recovery and lower systemic inflammation, but it remains nonspecific to renal perfusion/injury. As a medication-resumption proxy, it is unlikely to be a strong driver of Hour-60 creatinine ratio.
Bisacodyl use reflects bowel regimen/constipation prophylaxis and nursing practice rather than renal physiology. It has essentially no mechanistic connection to AKI or creatinine rise.
Bisacodyl administration is a routine supportive-care marker with high proxy penalty and negligible renal specificity. It should not meaningfully predict creatinine ratio.
Later bisacodyl use continues to represent bowel care and patient wakefulness/feeding status, not kidney injury. Any association would be incidental and weak.

Bivalirudin use suggests heparin avoidance (e.g., HIT concern) or complex anticoagulation, which can correlate with higher acuity. Renal relevance is indirect and early postoperative use is more a treatment-context proxy than an AKI mechanism.

Ongoing bivalirudin may indicate persistent critical illness or thrombosis/ECMO-related contexts that can increase AKI risk. Still, it is not kidney-specific and largely reflects clinician-driven anticoagulation strategy.

Bivalirudin in the 25–36h window can proxy ongoing severe illness/advanced support where renal hypoperfusion and AKI are more likely. Temporal proximity improves usefulness, but it remains an indirect marker rather than a direct renal signal.

Bumetanide use reflects diuretic response/volume management and may signal early oliguria, congestion, or attempts to manage low urine output. In the immediate postoperative period it is somewhat informative but confounded by routine fluid shifts and clinician behavior.

Loop diuretic exposure often reflects oliguria/volume overload and is tightly linked to evolving AKI management after cardiac surgery. In the 13–24h trajectory window it can be a strong (though partly clinician-driven) signal of worsening renal function or congestion.

Loop diuretic dosing close to the outcome window is a strong leading indicator of persistent oliguria/diuretic resistance and cardiorenal physiology. Still partly a treatment-choice proxy, but timing (25–36h) increases prognostic value for Hour-60 creatinine rise.

Calcium acetate is a phosphate binder typically used in advanced CKD/ESRD, reflecting low renal reserve rather than acute injury. In the immediate postop window it is mostly a chronic-disease/medication-history proxy with limited short-term signal.

Ongoing phosphate binder use suggests significant baseline CKD and may modestly stratify risk for postoperative creatinine rise. It is not a direct AKI biomarker and is largely a continuation-of-home-meds signal.

Phosphate binder administration near 36h still mainly encodes underlying CKD/ESRD status rather than acute renal perfusion or injury. Temporal proximity helps little because the drug is not mechanistically tied to short-term creatinine changes.
Calcium carbonate is commonly used for supplementation or as an antacid/binder and is not specific to renal injury. In early postop care it is largely nonspecific and clinician-practice driven.
Continued calcium carbonate dosing is a weak, nonspecific marker (supplement/antacid) with minimal linkage to AKI pathways. It carries high proxy/maintenance-care signal and low renal specificity.
Near the outcome window, calcium carbonate remains a nonspecific supportive medication and does not track renal perfusion or injury. Predictive value for creatinine_ratio is likely minimal.

IV calcium chloride is often given for post-bypass hypocalcemia, hyperkalemia treatment, or hemodynamic support, reflecting acute instability more than kidney injury. In 0–12h it is common and transient, so renal prognostic specificity is limited.

Calcium chloride use in 13–24h may indicate ongoing electrolyte derangements (e.g., hyperkalemia) or instability that can accompany AKI, but it is indirect and treatment-driven. Moderate proxy penalty keeps importance low.

Late calcium chloride administration can signal clinically significant hyperkalemia or persistent critical illness, which is more temporally aligned with evolving AKI by Hour 60. Still indirect and clinician-mediated, so not top-tier.

IV calcium gluconate is commonly used for hypocalcemia or hyperkalemia stabilization and reflects early postoperative physiology rather than renal injury per se. High frequency in 0–12h and treatment-choice effects reduce specificity for creatinine_ratio.

Use in 13–24h may correlate with electrolyte abnormalities that can accompany AKI, but it remains an indirect marker with substantial clinician-behavior influence. Temporal proximity is moderate but renal specificity is low.

Calcium gluconate given in 25–36h can be a closer-in-time signal of hyperkalemia management or ongoing metabolic derangement, which often co-tracks with worsening renal function. It is still not a direct kidney biomarker, limiting the ceiling.

Carvedilol is a chronic beta-blocker and early postop administration mainly reflects home-med continuation or blood pressure/heart rate management. It has minimal direct linkage to AKI mechanisms and high practice-pattern confounding.

Beta-blocker dosing in 13–24h is largely a cardiovascular management variable and not renal-specific. Any association with creatinine_ratio would be indirect via hemodynamics and is likely weak compared with direct renal/perfusion markers.

Carvedilol is a beta/alpha-blocker reflecting chronic HF/HTN management and clinician choice rather than renal injury. It may weakly relate to hemodynamics/perfusion but is not a direct AKI pathway signal even in the closest window.

Cefepime use early post-op is mainly a marker of suspected infection/prophylaxis and care intensity; it is renally cleared but not typically nephrotoxic. The 0–12h timing is also less informative for Hour-60 creatinine trajectory.

Cefepime dosing in this window may proxy evolving infection/sepsis risk (which can contribute to AKI) but remains indirect and treatment-driven. Renal relevance is modest and largely confounded by indication and dose adjustment for kidney function.

Later cefepime dosing can reflect ongoing infection/sepsis physiology closer to the outcome window and may also encode renal dose-adjustment behavior as kidney function worsens. Still indirect and clinician-mediated, so not top-tier.

Ceftriaxone exposure is usually prophylaxis/empiric coverage and is not a strong nephrotoxin. In the immediate post-op window it is mostly a nonspecific care-process marker with little direct linkage to Hour-60 creatinine ratio.
Ceftriaxone use here remains largely an infection-treatment proxy and is not tightly mechanistic for AKI. Binary exposure adds limited granularity and is heavily clinician-driven.

Persistent ceftriaxone use closer to Hour 60 may indicate ongoing infection/inflammation that can worsen renal function, but the drug itself is not strongly nephrotoxic. Predictive value is therefore indirect and modest despite favorable timing.

Clonidine is an antihypertensive/sedative adjunct that can lower sympathetic tone and blood pressure, potentially affecting renal perfusion. However, early post-op dosing is mostly practice-pattern driven and not a specific AKI marker.
